summary: Battery & stress-test the Kamikakushi PRD from 14 audit lenses; adversarially verify each finding; synthesize a deduped findings report splitting trivial-fixes from human-questions
logs: Raw findings: 78 · confirmed after adversarial verify: 60

confirmedFindings.title: §4.8.1 T0 pacing table: per-rung Arms-deed counts cannot produce the stated Arms ip or the claimed 30-Arms total (Arms-accrual onset vs cadence are internally inconsistent)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §4.8.1 R5 row (line 2959): "| **R5 Gate-guard** ... | **first DEFEND deed (20 ip)** + ~6 Arms deeds (Arms→~0.25K); ~5 Estate deeds; Combat Standing x2 | ~80 koku/min | **~40 min** |". The table also asserts Arms deeds only START at R5 (line 2959: "**Arms deeds BEGIN accruing**"; standing recorded at R6) yet claims the T0 total is "**30 Estate + 30 Arms** = ~56–60 total recognised deeds, one act every ~4.5–5 min (**≈9–10 min per individual pillar**)" (line 2966). The authoritative §4.2.1 itemization (lines 2412-2413) makes T0 Arms = 350 ip from 30 deeds (20 minor@8 + 5 road@18 + 5 defend@20). Arithmetic: ~6 Arms deeds at T0 bases (8–20 ip) yields at most ~120 ip, not the stated ~0.25K (250 ip); and the visible per-rung Arms counts (R5 ~6 + R6 ~5 ≈ 11, R7 unspecified) cannot reach 30. Confining all 30 Arms deeds to R5–R7 (~135 min) also contradicts the table's own "≈9–10 min per individual pillar" even-cadence claim (that implies ~4.5 min/Arms-deed in the back third).
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: §4.8.1 is billed as the centrepiece "full resolution" table the rest of §4 is tuned to, and the M6 balance pass + the §6.10 headless pacing/deed-cadence regression key off it. The Arms column under-specifies and mis-states the numbers a tuner would build to, so the pacing test target and the table disagree. The Estate side ties out cleanly; only Arms is back-loaded and inconsistent.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Decide how the 30 Arms deeds (350 ip) distribute across R5→R7 given Arms accrual begins at R5, then correct the R5 'Arms→~0.25K' figure and enumerate R7's Arms-deed count so the per-rung counts visibly sum to ~30; reconcile the 'evenly ~9–10 min per pillar' phrasing with Arms being concentrated in R5–R7 (~4.5 min/Arms-deed there).
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Confirmed verbatim: §4.8.1 R5 row (line 2959) states "~6 Arms deeds (Arms→~0.25K)" with Arms deeds BEGINNING at R5. The §4.2.1 deed bases (lines 2392-2394) cap T0 Arms deeds at 8/18/20 ip, so ~6 deeds yield at most ~120 ip — not the 250 ip the "Arms→~0.25K" snapshot implies. Total T0 Arms seasonal stream is only 150 ip (line 2968) and Arms standing isn't recorded until R6, so seasonal cannot legitimately front-load ~130+ ip into R5 to close the gap. This is a real internal inconsistency, isolated to the Arms column while Estate ties out exactly (lines 2409-2413). HOWEVER, one prong of the finding is overstated: R7's Arms-deed count is simply not enumerated, so the per-rung counts CAN sum to 30 (R7 ≈ 19) — the table never claims they must visibly sum. Downgraded medium→low because the figures are explicitly "proposed" (only the ≥30-min floor and 4.5h sum are LOCKED) and the strongest-sounding part of the complaint ("cannot reach 30") does not hold. Remaining real defect is a balance-distribution call (how to spread 30 Arms deeds / fix the R5 snapshot), so needs-human.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">In §4.8.1's T0 table, Arms deeds begin at R5 yet the end state is 30 Arms deeds = 350 deed-ip (+150 seasonal = 0.5K). Two things don't tie out: (1) R5's "~6 Arms deeds → Arms ~0.25K" — 6 deeds at T0 bases (8-20 ip, §4.2.1) give at most ~120 ip, and total Arms seasonal is only 150 ip for all of T0, so 250 ip by end of R5 is unreachable; (2) the per-rung Arms counts shown (R5 ~6, R6 ~5) leave ~19 Arms deeds implicitly in R7, contradicting the "evenly ~9-10 min per pillar" cadence (Arms is concentrated in R5-R7 ≈ 4.5 min/deed). How should the 30 Arms deeds (350 ip) distribute across R5/R6/R7, and what is R5's correct Arms snapshot — is "~0.25K" a typo for a lower figure (e.g. ~0.1K), or should the deed counts/onset shift earlier? (Estate ties out exactly; only Arms needs reconciling.)
confirmedFindings.question: §4.8.1 says Arms deeds begin at R5 but the T0 total must be 30 Arms deeds / 350 ip. How should the 30 Arms deeds spread across R5/R6/R7 (and is R5's 'Arms→~0.25K' the intended snapshot, since ~6 deeds at 8–20 ip can only give ~120 ip)? This is a balance-distribution call, not a clerical fix.

confirmedFindings.title: prd_human_feedback.md H2 still marked deferred (⏳) although J4's §5 authenticity pass is RESOLVED and now fully applied in prd.md
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd_human_feedback.md line 265 (H2) vs lines 321-324 (J4) and prd.md lines 378, 386, 1805, 423
confirmedFindings.evidence: H2 (line 265) reads: "- **H2. §5 authenticity pass kept deferred** (Standing & Office kanji + martial/office titles → a later focused pass). ⏳". But J4 (line 321) marks it "**RESOLVED (2026-06-25)**" and the resolutions ARE now in prd.md — confirmed by grep: 官威 *kan'i* (lines 378, 386), R7 = *jikata-yaku* (line 1805), *michi-ban* diegetic-only (line 3840), and the T4 *rusui-yaku* reframe (line 423). The intent doc's own H2 status marker is therefore stale (still ⏳ for work that is done and applied).
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: This file is declared 'the record of intent'; a future auditor reading H2 in isolation would wrongly conclude the §5 authenticity pass is still pending. The PRD itself is correct — this is purely a stale status marker in the intent record.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Update H2's marker to ✅ and point it at J4, noting the pass is applied in prd.md.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified in docs/prd_human_feedback.md: H2 (lines 264-265) still reads "§5 authenticity pass kept deferred ... ⏳", but J4 (lines 321-324) marks the identical pass "RESOLVED (2026-06-25)" and grep confirms it is fully applied in prd.md — 官威 *kan'i* (378, 386, 1436, 1702, 753, 801), R7 *jikata-yaku* (225, 1805), *michi-ban* diegetic-only (238, 1908, 3840), and the T4 *rusui-yaku* reframe (423, 470, 1464, 3838-3852). The two markers in the same intent doc contradict each other; H2's ⏳ is stale. Pure clerical status-marker fix, no design trade-off. NOTE: the fix lives in docs/prd_human_feedback.md (not prd.md, which is already correct); verified_old/new below are byte-exact from prd_human_feedback.md lines 264-265.
confirmedFindings.old_string:
- **H2. §5 authenticity pass kept deferred** (Standing & Office kanji + martial/office titles → a later
  focused pass). ⏳
confirmedFindings.new_string: - **H2. §5 authenticity pass** (Standing & Office kanji + martial/office titles). ✅ **RESOLVED & APPLIED 2026-06-25 (superseded by J4):** 官威 *kan'i*, R7 *jikata-yaku*, *michi-ban* diegetic-only, T4 *rusui-yaku* — all now in prd.md (§1.6.1, §3.2, §1.7).

confirmedFindings.title: R6/E2 require Estate Influence ≥ 1K, which EXCEEDS the T0→T1 Estate gate of 0.8K (the very next rung) — impossible on an up-only curve
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §4.8.1 line 2960 (R6) and §4.7.5 line 2904 (E2); vs §4.1 line 2316 / §4.8.1 line 2961 (T0→T1 gate)
confirmedFindings.evidence: R6 (line 2960): "**2K koku** (build E2); **Estate ≥ 1K** + Arms ≥ 0.4K". E2 gate (line 2904): "**Estate ≥ 1K** + Arms ≥ 0.4K, rank ≥ R6". But the T0→T1 gate one rung LATER (R7, line 2961) is "**Arms ≥ 0.5K · Estate ≥ 0.8K** (the §4.1 gate)", and §4.1 line 2316 = "Estate ≥ 0.8K ip". The §4.2.1/§4.2.2 accrual delivers EXACTLY 0.8K Estate over all of T0 (560 deed-ip + 240 seasonal-ip = 800), reached only at the R7 capstone (final autumn, season 8). Because Estate is up-only/monotone, the player hits the 0.8K tier gate BEFORE ever reaching 1K — so R6 can never be left as written, and the 1K floor sits above both the gate (0.8K) and the displayed band (~1K). Note Arms is set correctly: R6 floor 0.4K = 80% of its 0.5K R7 gate; Estate is the anomaly (1K > 0.8K). At R6 the intended Estate value is only ~0.6K (≈24/30 deeds + 6/8 seasonal).
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: As written, the intended T0 progression is logically impossible — a player on the tuned curve hits the tier-up gate while still 'stuck' at R6/E2, and a CI pacing test driving the spine would deadlock.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Lower the E2/R6 Estate Influence floor to a value strictly below the 0.8K T0→T1 gate (E1 uses 0.3K; by analogy with Arms' 0.4K = 0.8×gate, Estate ≈ 0.5–0.6K). Apply identically at line 2904 and line 2960.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Confirmed verbatim in both locations. §4.7.5 E2 gate = "Estate ≥ 1K + Arms ≥ 0.4K, rank ≥ R6" and the §4.8.1 R6 rung repeats "Estate ≥ 1K + Arms ≥ 0.4K". But the §4.1 T0→T1 gate (filled at the LATER R7 capstone) is Estate ≥ 0.8K, and the §4.8.1 R7 totals line explicitly states the T0 Estate curve maxes at exactly 0.8K ("Pillars at the R7 gate: Estate = 0.8K = 560 deed-ip + 240 seasonal-ip"). Since Estate is up-only and never exceeds 0.8K anywhere in T0, requiring 1K to build E2 at the earlier R6 rung is unreachable — E2 is unbuildable within T0 and R6 is unleaveable on the tuned curve; a CI pacing test driving the spine would deadlock. The R6 Arms floor (0.4K = 80% of its 0.5K gate) is consistent; only the Estate 1K floor is the anomaly (1K > the 0.8K gate and > the ~0.5–0.6K Estate value implied by the R6 "~6 Estate deeds" narrative). Not reconciled anywhere else. Real, high severity. Needs-human because the corrected value is a balance judgement and must be applied identically in two coupled places (§4.7.5 line ~2904 and §4.8.1 line ~2960).</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">The E2/R6 building gate requires Estate Influence ≥ 1K, but Estate accrual over all of T0 tops out at exactly 0.8K (the T0→T1 gate, reached only at the final rung R7 per §4.8.1's own totals). On an up-only curve the player can never reach 1K within T0, so E2 is unbuildable and R6 is unleaveable. What should the E2/R6 Estate floor be? Arms' R6 floor mirrors its gate at 80% (0.4K = 0.8×0.5K); mirroring that for Estate gives 0.64K (≈0.5–0.6K, consistent with the ~6-Estate-deeds-by-R6 narrative). This must be changed identically in §4.7.5 (E2 row) and §4.8.1 (R6 row).</refined_question>
</invoke>
confirmedFindings.question: E2/R6 require Estate Influence ≥ 1K, but the next rung (R7) is the T0→T1 gate at Estate ≥ 0.8K, and §4.2.1/§4.2.2 deliver exactly 0.8K Estate over all of T0 (only reached at R7). On an up-only curve the player reaches the 0.8K gate before 1K, so R6 is unleaveable. What should the E2/R6 Estate floor be? (Arms' R6 floor is 0.4K = 80% of its 0.5K gate; mirroring that gives Estate ≈ 0.5–0.6K.)

confirmedFindings.title: §4.9 levers index lists Name JUDGE_K as 3,000 (T1) / 24,000 (T2), contradicting the §4.2.2 derivation of 4,500 / 42,000
confirmedFindings.evidence: §4.9 (lines 3055–3056): "...Office 600 / Name **3,000** · T2 Arms 9,000 / Estate 18,000 / Office 15,000 / Name **24,000**". But §4.2.2 states JUDGE_K[Name] = 0.30·TIER_REF_NAME with TIER_REF_NAME = armsGate+estateGate+officeGate = **15K (T1) / 140K (T2)** (line 2502), giving Name T1 = 0.30·15,000 = **4,500** and T2 = 0.30·140,000 = **42,000** — exactly what the §4.2.2 table (line 2510) lists. The Arms/Estate/Office values in §4.9 all match §4.2.2; only Name is stale (3,000 = 0.30·10,000 and 24,000 = 0.30·80,000 do not derive from any current TIER_REF_NAME). §4.9's own stated formula 'JUDGE_K = SEASONAL_SHARE · gate' would yield 4,500/42,000.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: An implementer reading the levers 'dashboard' (§4.9) rather than §4.2.2 would under-fund the Name seasonal stream by ~⅓, breaking the stated 0.30·TIER_REF_NAME invariant; the two tables for the same constant must agree.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Update the §4.9 Name JUDGE_K values to match the authoritative §4.2.2 derivation (4,500 / 42,000).
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed real internal inconsistency. §4.9 (docs/prd.md line 3055-3056) lists the derived JUDGE_K table with Name = 3,000 (T1) / 24,000 (T2). The authoritative §4.2.2 table (line 2510) lists Name = 4,500 (T1) / 42,000 (T2), derived from TIER_REF_NAME = 15K (T1) / 140K (T2) (line 2502) times SEASONAL_SHARE = 0.30. The §4.9 Arms/Estate/Office values (9,000/18,000/15,000 for T2; 1,500/2,400/600 for T1) all match §4.2.2 exactly; only the Name values are stale — 3,000 = 0.30·10,000 and 24,000 = 0.30·80,000 correspond to retired TIER_REF_NAME values, not the current 15K/140K. §4.9's own stated formula 'JUDGE_K = SEASONAL_SHARE · gate' yields 4,500/42,000. This is a clerical table-sync with no design judgement: the §4.2.2 derivation is unambiguously authoritative and self-consistent, so the §4.9 dashboard values are simply stale duplicates. Severity medium (an implementer reading only the §4.9 dashboard would under-fund the Name seasonal stream by ~1/3, breaking the 0.30·TIER_REF_NAME invariant).

confirmedFindings.title: §4.5.1 XP-curve example figures (L10 ~2.1K, L25 ~41K, L50 ~1.5M) do not match the stated formula (XP_BASE=50, XP_GROWTH=1.18)
confirmedFindings.evidence: Line 2693: "`XP_BASE = 50`, `XP_GROWTH = 1.18`. So L1→L2 costs 50 xp, L10 cumulatively ~**2.1K**, L25 ~**41K**, L50 ~**1.5M** xp." Computing totalXpForLevel(L) = Σ round(50·1.18^(i-1)) from the stated formula gives L10 = 1,176 (~1.2K), L25 = 17,130 (~17K), L50 = 1,090,653 (~1.1M). The stated examples are off by 1.78× / 2.4× / 1.38× (inconsistent factors, i.e. not a uniform rescale — the '~41K' is actually the L30 value, 39,546). Orders of magnitude vs the §4.0 koku spine remain fine; only the illustrative numbers are wrong.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: These figures are cited as the sanity-check that the XP curve scales with the tier soft-caps; if the M6 balance pass trusts the stated numbers instead of the formula it will mis-judge the curve. Low impact because XP is not a gate currency and the formula is authoritative.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Recompute the illustrative cumulative-XP figures from the stated formula: L10 ~1.2K, L25 ~17K, L50 ~1.1M.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed by recomputing the stated formula. totalXpForLevel(L) = Σ round(50·1.18^(i-1)) gives L10=1,176 (~1.2K), L25=17,130 (~17K), L50=1,090,653 (~1.1M), versus the PRD's stated ~2.1K / ~41K / ~1.5M. The formula at lines 2688-2690 is explicitly authoritative and the constants (XP_BASE=50, XP_GROWTH=1.18) are stated; the prose figures are merely illustrative and are simply miscalculated. The '~41K' even matches the L30 value (39,546), confirming a calculation slip rather than a different intended curve. Correcting derived illustrative numbers to match the stated formula is purely clerical with no balance/design trade-off (XP is not a gate currency; the formula stands). Verified text exists verbatim.

confirmedFindings.title: Arms ≥ 30K is a hard required gate at T2→T3, but §4.8.3 and §1.6.3 frame the T2 required pillars as 'Estate + Office' with 'Arms secures roads'
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §4.1 line 2318 & §4.8.3-G7 line 3018 (gates) vs §4.8.3 header line 3007 & §1.6.3 line 421 (framing)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §4.1 (line 2318) T2→T3 gate: "Estate ≥ 60K · Office ≥ 50K · **Arms ≥ 30K**"; §4.8.3 G7 (line 3018) repeats "Estate ≥ 60K · Office ≥ 50K · **Arms ≥ 30K** (§4.1 v1 end-gate)". Yet §4.8.3's header (line 3007) says "Required pillars drift to **Estate + Office** (Arms secures roads)" and §1.6.3 (line 421) lists the T2 required pillars as "**Estate + Office** rising; **Arms** secures roads". So Arms is a third hard threshold at the v1 end-gate, but the narrative framing presents it as supporting, not required.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The two framings disagree on whether a player can reach T3 with Arms below 30K. A reader following the 'drift to Estate+Office' description could under-build Arms and stall at the unstated 30K gate.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Decide and state consistently: if Arms ≥ 30K is a hard co-gate at T2→T3 (as both gate tables say), tighten the 'drift to Estate+Office / Arms secures roads' prose in §4.8.3 and §1.6.3 to note Arms ≥ 30K is still a required threshold (3 required pillars at the v1 end-gate).
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed verbatim. §4.1 (line 2318) and §4.8.3 G7 (line 3018) both list T2→T3 as three independent thresholds including Arms ≥ 30K, and §4.8.3's own deed/seasonal budget fully derives Arms 30K (21,000 deed ip + 9,000 seasonal). The narrative prose in §4.8.3 header (line 3007, "Required pillars drift to Estate + Office (Arms secures roads)") and §1.6.3 (line 421, "Estate + Office rising; Arms secures roads") implies only two required pillars, so a reader following the prose could under-build Arms and stall at the unstated 30K gate. The contradiction is genuine but the resolution direction is NOT an open design question: the authoritative gate tables and the deed budget both treat Arms 30K as a fully-required co-gate, so the only consistent fix is to tighten the prose (removing Arms from the gates would silently void the §4.8.3 21K-deed Arms budget). That makes it a clerical alignment with no balance trade-off. The verified edit targets the §4.8.3 header sitting directly above the contradicting G7 row; the parallel §1.6.3 line 421 phrase ("Estate + Office rising; Arms secures roads") should receive the same tightening for full consistency.
confirmedFindings.old_string: Required pillars drift to **Estate + Office** (Arms secures roads). Each rung ≥ ~75 min; capstone among the longest.
confirmedFindings.new_string: Required pillars drift toward **Estate + Office** (rising), with **Arms ≥ 30K** still a required co-gate at the v1 end-gate (Arms secures roads). Each rung ≥ ~75 min; capstone among the longest.
confirmedFindings.question: At T2→T3 (the v1 end-gate), is Arms ≥ 30K a hard co-required threshold (3 required pillars, as §4.1 and §4.8.3-G7 both list) or merely supporting (the 'Arms secures roads' / 'drift to Estate+Office' framing)? If hard, the descriptive prose should stop implying only 2 required pillars; if supporting, Arms should be removed from the gate tables.

confirmedFindings.title: hpMax and the attribute-point economy depend on 'characterLevel', but no character-level XP curve/accrual is defined (only per-skill curves) — and the term collides with 'Combat Level' / 'combatLevel'
confirmedFindings.evidence: §4.6.1: "hpMax = 40 + 8·characterLevel + 2·STR + gearHp". §4.4: "**Attribute points:** **+1 point every 2 character levels**". §2.7: "Character level + XP grants HP, satiety capacity, attribute points...". But §4.5 (the only XP-curve section) defines exclusively PER-SKILL curves: "xpForLevel(L) = round( XP_BASE * XP_GROWTH^(L-1) )" for skills — there is NO character-level curve, no statement of what feeds character XP, nor its value at any rank (R3's characterLevel is unstated). Separately, §2.8 calls it "a **character Combat Level** (a Combat Deeds pool: combat-XP + a deed-count...)" and §4.3 uses "combatLevel" — so it is unclear whether characterLevel (HP/attr-points), Combat Level, and combatLevel are one quantity or three.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: hpMax is a core combat derived stat and the attribute-point economy gates every build; both are uncomputable because the 'character level' progression is undefined. This is also why finding combat-math-1 is doubly unverifiable (player HP at R3 is indeterminate). The term collision invites an implementer to wire HP to the wrong level source.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Define the character-level curve and its XP source in §4.5 (or §2.7), state characterLevel at each T0 rank, and disambiguate characterLevel vs Combat Level vs combatLevel (pick one canonical name per concept).
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed against source. hpMax (§4.6.1 l.2765) and the attribute-point cadence (§4.4 l.2661: "+1 point every 2 character levels") both depend on `characterLevel`, but §4.5 — the only XP-curve section — defines exclusively per-skill geometric curves (`xpForLevel(L)`), with no character-level curve and no statement of what feeds character XP. The gap is stronger than the auditor claimed: the canonical stored GameState in §6.4 (l.4048) stores only `character: { hp, satiety, attributePoints, attributes }` — there is NO character-level or character-xp field, so `characterLevel` is neither stored nor derivable from any defined input (skills store their own xp; attributePoints is a stored counter with no defined grant trigger). §2.7(c) l.1077 lists `character Level + xp` but §6.4 dropped it, so the two sections also disagree. The locked canon (brainstorms/2026-06-25-locked-decisions.md:106) names only "character combat level + skills," confirming the general "character level" the PRD hangs HP and the build economy on was introduced without a curve/source. Term collision is also real: "character Combat Level" (§2.8 l.1098), `combatLevel` (§4.3 l.2621), and `characterLevel` (§4.6.1/§4.4) are three spellings with no statement of whether they are one quantity or distinct. High severity (player HP and every build gate are uncomputable); needs-human because defining the curve/source and picking canonical names are design/balance decisions, not clerical.
confirmedFindings.question: "Character level" is load-bearing for two core systems — hpMax (§4.6.1: 40 + 8·characterLevel + …) and the attribute-point economy (§4.4: +1 point per 2 character levels) — but it is never defined: §4.5 gives only per-skill XP curves, §6.4's stored GameState has no character-xp/level field, and canon only ever named a "character COMBAT level." Three questions: (1) How is character level earned and curved — does it get its own XP pool + curve (and what feeds it: a slice of all action XP? deeds?), or should HP/attribute-points instead key off an already-defined quantity (e.g. the Combat Level from the Combat Deeds pool, or an aggregate of skill levels)? (2) What is characterLevel at R3 (needed to compute player HP for the humbling first fight)? (3) Are `characterLevel` (HP/attr), the "character Combat Level" (§2.8, Combat Deeds → Arms), and `combatLevel` (§4.3 pillar conversion) one concept or separate — pick one canonical name per distinct concept and add the stored field to §6.4. Trade-off: a separate character-XP track adds a counter to balance and store; reusing Combat Level couples HP/build progression to combat activity (cuts against the peaceful-labour-dominant texture).

confirmedFindings.title: INT's and SPD's stated combat effects in §4.4 are not wired into any §4.6 combat formula — INT is a combat dump-stat in the actual math
confirmedFindings.evidence: §4.4 claims INT has combat value: "`+0.5%` damage vs known bestiary entries; better stance/ability effects", and SPD: "...turn-order/first-strike". But the §4.6 combat formulas contain no INT term and no turn-order/first-strike mechanic: damage is "rawDamage = attackPower · rngVariance(0.85 … 1.15)" then "damage = max( DAMAGE_FLOOR, rawDamage − defense )" — INT appears nowhere; the sim is a sub-tick accumulator with no first-strike/turn-order step. So INT contributes zero in combat per §4.6, contradicting §4.4 and the §4.4 promise that every attribute "is never a dump stat" (and the no-respec rule that leans on that promise).
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: A player who invests in INT for the advertised '+0.5% damage vs known bestiary' gets nothing in combat, and with no respec (LOCKED) that mis-investment is permanent. The §4.4↔§4.6 mismatch means the combat model is not self-consistent on which attributes feed damage.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Either add the INT bestiary-damage multiplier (and a SPD first-strike rule) to the §4.6 formulas, or strike those combat effects from §4.4 and re-state how INT/SPD avoid being combat dump-stats.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Partially real, with the SPD half refuted. SPD's main §4.4 combat effect IS wired into §4.6: §4.6.1 has `attackSpeed = baseSpeed · (1 + 0.005·SPD) · stance.speedMod` and §4.6.2 is a full SPD-cadence subsection; the only missing SPD clause is "turn-order/first-strike", which is N/A by design (the sim is a sub-tick accumulator with no turns). The INT half is real: §4.4 line 2655 promises INT "+0.5% damage vs known bestiary entries; better stance/ability effects", and INT appears in none of the §4.6.1–§4.6.4 formulas. However the gap is mitigated: §4.4's combat column is explicitly tagged "*(proposed)*" while §4.6 is the "fixed" math (incompleteness, not a true contradiction), and §4.5.3 already self-states "INT/SPD/LUCK's combat effects are minor/incidental." The "combat dump-stat / permanent mis-investment" framing is overblown — INT carries strong labour effects (craft quality, recipe unlocks, admin deed weight), so it is not a dump stat under the no-dump-stat contract. Net: a low-severity §4.4↔§4.6 spec gap for INT only; resolving it is a balance/design decision, hence needs-human.</parameter>
<parameter name="refined_question">INT's §4.4 combat effect ("+0.5% damage vs known bestiary entries; better stance/ability effects") has no corresponding term anywhere in the §4.6 combat math, while §4.5.3 already calls INT/SPD/LUCK combat effects "minor/incidental." Do you want to (a) add a concrete INT term to §4.6 (e.g. a bestiary-knowledge damage multiplier in §4.6.3 and an INT input to stance/ability magnitudes), or (b) strike INT's combat-effect cell from the §4.4 table and let INT be a labour-only attribute (its no-dump-stat justification resting entirely on its labour effects)? Note: SPD is already wired (attackSpeed in §4.6.1), so only its "turn-order/first-strike" phrase — which the turnless sub-tick sim doesn't support — would need to be dropped from §4.4 for consistency.</parameter>
<parameter name="verified_old">
confirmedFindings.question: Should INT's '+0.5% damage vs known bestiary entries' and SPD's 'turn-order/first-strike' be implemented as real terms in the §4.6 combat math (and where in the damage pipeline), or removed from §4.4? As written they are promised in §4.4 but absent from every §4.6 formula, making INT a combat dump-stat despite the no-dump-stat / no-respec locks.

confirmedFindings.title: On-kill Combat-XP formula uses 'mobLevel', but MobDef (§2.9c) has no level field — undefined variable
confirmedFindings.evidence: §4.6.5: "**Combat XP** = `mobLevel · COMBAT_XP_K`, then per-event capped (§4.5.2). `COMBAT_XP_K = 12`". The MobDef data shape in §2.9(c) is "`MobDef { id, kind (...), dangerRing, stats (Combatant base), lootTableId, spawnWeightByRing, isGrindable (true), bestiaryEntryId }`" — it has no `level` field. So `mobLevel` is referenced by the XP formula but never defined in the mob data model (nor derived from dangerRing anywhere).
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Combat XP per kill cannot be computed because its only input variable is undefined in the content model. The audit criterion 'no undefined variable' fails here, and the XP→character-level loop (already shaky per combat-math-2) inherits the gap.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add a `level` field to MobDef (and to the bestiary stat blocks) or define mobLevel as a function of dangerRing, and reference it from §4.6.5.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified in docs/prd.md. §4.6.5 (line 2798) defines Combat XP = `mobLevel · COMBAT_XP_K`, but `mobLevel` occurs only at that line (grep confirms a single hit). The MobDef data shape (§2.9c, line 1159) lists id, kind, dangerRing, stats (Combatant base), lootTableId, spawnWeightByRing, isGrindable, bestiaryEntryId — no `level` field. The Combatant model it embeds (§2.8c line 1116; §4.6.1 lines 2757-2766) has hp/attackPower/attackSpeed/evasion/defense/critChance/blockChance/statuses — also no level. The only level-like fields in the PRD are the MC's `characterLevel` (line 2765) and `combatLevel` (line 2621), neither of which applies to a mob. So the XP-per-kill formula references a variable that has no definition or derivation anywhere. This is a genuine dangling-ref/undefined-variable gap. Severity stays medium: it sits in a §4 lever/tunable section (COMBAT_XP_K is even marked *(proposed)*) and does not contradict any LOCKED canon, but the XP formula is uncomputable as written. Not trivial-apply: resolving it requires a design choice (carry an explicit authored `level` on MobDef, vs derive mobLevel from dangerRing, vs some other proxy), each with balance/content-model consequences — a real trade-off, hence needs-human.
confirmedFindings.question: Combat XP per kill (§4.6.5) is defined as `mobLevel · COMBAT_XP_K`, but `mobLevel` is never defined: MobDef (§2.9c) carries `dangerRing` and `stats (Combatant base)` but no `level`, and the Combatant shape has no level either. How should a mob's level be sourced for the XP formula? Options: (a) add an explicit authored `level` field to MobDef (most control, more to hand-author per bestiary entry); (b) derive `mobLevel` from `dangerRing` (e.g. a ring→level mapping — fewer authored numbers, ties XP to the existing danger gradient); or (c) derive it from the mob's stat block. Whichever is chosen, §4.6.5 should reference the defined source instead of a bare `mobLevel`.

confirmedFindings.title: §4.6.2 cadence: 'baseSpeed = 1.0 swing per 20 sub-ticks at SPD 5' contradicts the attackSpeed formula (1.0 base is at SPD 0; SPD 5 gives 1.025)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §4.6.1: "attackSpeed = baseSpeed · (1 + 0.005·SPD) · stance.speedMod" with baseSpeed = 1.0 — so at SPD 0 attackSpeed = 1.0 and at SPD 5 it is 1.0·(1+0.025) = 1.025. But §4.6.2 says "`baseSpeed = 1.0` swing per **20 combat sub-ticks** at SPD 5", attributing the unmodified 1.0 to SPD 5 rather than SPD 0 (off by the +0.5%·SPD multiplier).
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Minor, but the cadence anchor (how many sub-ticks a swing costs) is stated against the wrong SPD value, which an implementer calibrating the sub-tick accumulator could carry into a slightly-off baseline.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Tie the 1.0/20-sub-tick anchor to SPD 0 (the formula's base), noting the starting SPD-5 character swings ~1.025× that.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed: §4.6.1 (line 2759) defines attackSpeed = baseSpeed·(1 + 0.005·SPD)·stance.speedMod, so the (1 + 0.005·SPD) factor is exactly 1.0 only at SPD 0 (and 1.025 at SPD 5). §4.6.2 (lines 2770-2771) states `baseSpeed = 1.0` swing per 20 sub-ticks "at SPD 5", which mis-attributes the unmodified base value to SPD 5 instead of SPD 0. Genuine internal inconsistency; the formula makes baseSpeed the SPD-0 anchor unambiguously, so the correction is clerical with no design/balance judgement.
confirmedFindings.old_string: sub-ticks** at SPD 5; each point of SPD shaves cadence per the `+0.5%·SPD` above.
confirmedFindings.new_string: sub-ticks** at SPD 0 (the `(1 + 0.005·SPD)` factor is 1.0 at SPD 0, ≈1.025 at the starting SPD 5); each point of SPD shaves cadence per the `+0.5%·SPD` above.

confirmedFindings.title: §4.4 STR row omits STR's defense and hpMax contributions that §4.6.1 actually implements
confirmedFindings.evidence: §4.4's STR combat-effect cell lists only "`attackPower += 1.2·STR` (melee); raises carry/durability damage". But §4.6.1 gives STR two further combat roles: "defense = gearDef + 0.5·STR + stance.defMod" and "hpMax = 40 + 8·characterLevel + 2·STR + gearHp". The attribute table (the player-facing/derivation summary) therefore under-states STR's combat footprint.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The §4.4 table is meant to be the single source of truth for what each attribute does; an incomplete STR row can mislead balance tuning and the build-planning the no-respec rule makes load-bearing.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add STR's defense and hpMax contributions to the §4.4 STR combat-effect cell, cross-referencing §4.6.1.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Verified at the cited lines. §4.4 STR combat cell (line 2653) lists only `attackPower += 1.2·STR` (melee) plus carry/durability flavour, but §4.6.1 (line 2762 `defense = gearDef + 0.5·STR + stance.defMod` and line 2765 `hpMax = 40 + 8·characterLevel + 2·STR + gearHp`) gives STR two more concrete combat contributions. The under-statement is real and breaks the table's own convention — AGI's and LUCK's cells DO enumerate their multiple §4.6.1 effects (evasion/crit/accuracy; crit/block/loot), so STR is the lone attribute whose secondary combat numbers are omitted. Fix is a pure documentation sync of values already specified in §4.6.1, so no design/balance judgement is needed.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="verified_old">| **STR** (力) | `attackPower += 1.2·STR` (melee); raises carry/durability damage | labour yield `+0.8%·STR`; hauling capacity `+2·STR`; lower stamina cost on heavy work |
confirmedFindings.old_string: `attackPower += 1.2·STR` (melee); raises carry/durability damage
confirmedFindings.new_string: | **STR** (力) | `attackPower += 1.2·STR` (melee); `defense += 0.5·STR`; `hpMax += 2·STR` (§4.6.1); raises carry/durability damage | labour yield `+0.8%·STR`; hauling capacity `+2·STR`; lower stamina cost on heavy work |

confirmedFindings.title: §3.6/§5 say T2 reaches "early E3"; §2.17/§7.1.2/§4.7.5 lock v1 at E0–E2 with E3–E5 parked
confirmedFindings.evidence: §3.6 line 2013: "Estate stage span: **E2 Recovering → early E3** (estate fabric runs *ahead* of top personal rank...)" and §5 T2.4 line 3541: "Estate roster & buildings growing (E2 → early E3)". But §2.17 line 1524: "**v1 covers stages E0–E2** (E0 Foreclosure's Edge → E1 Stabilising → E2 Recovering); **E3–E5 parked.**"; §7.1.2 line 4379: "**Estate stages** | **E0 → E1 → E2** ...; E3–E5 parked"; and §4.7.5 line 2895 only authors "Building / upgrade costs (estate stages E0→E2, v1)" — there are NO E3 build costs.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: A builder following the locked scope (§7.1.2) and the only authored cost table (§4.7.5) ships E0–E2; a reader of §3.6/§5 T2 expects the estate to tip into E3 during T2 — for which no cost/content exists. The two statements cannot both be true.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Decide whether v1's T2 formally reaches the start of E3 or stops at E2, then make §3.6/§5 T2 and §2.17/§7.1.2/§4.7.5 agree (e.g. drop "early E3" to "E2 Recovering", or add an E3 entry/cost and update the parked-list).
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Verified verbatim. docs/prd.md line 2013 (§3.6) and lines 3452/3541/3553 (§5 T2.4) state the estate fabric reaches "early E3" during T2 (e.g. 2013: "Estate stage span: **E2 Recovering → early E3**"; 3541 header "Estate roster & buildings growing (E2 → early E3)"). This directly conflicts with the LOCKED v1 scope: line 1524 (§2.17) "**v1 covers stages E0–E2** ... **E3–E5 parked.**"; line 4379 (§7.1.2) "**Estate stages** | **E0 → E1 → E2** ...; E3–E5 parked"; and §4.7.5's only authored cost table ends at "E2 Recovering" (line 2904), with line 2911 stating "E3–E5 parked (§1.5.2)". A builder following the locked scope and the only cost table ships E0–E2 with no E3 stage or cost, so the T2 sections promise a stage that has no content/cost. The contradiction is genuine. Not trivial-apply: resolving it is a scope/design decision (either extend v1 to author an E3 stage + §4.7.5 cost, or trim the T2 narrative to cap at E2), so needs-human.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">Does v1's T2 formally reach the start of E3 (requiring a new E3 estate stage entry plus a §4.7.5 build/upgrade cost, and removal of E3 from the parked list), or does v1 cap the estate fabric at E2 Recovering with E3–E5 fully parked? If the latter, the "early E3" phrasing in §3.6 (line 2013) and §5 T2.4 (lines 3452/3541/3553) must be dropped to "E2 Recovering". The two cannot both stand: §2.17/§7.1.2/§4.7.5 currently lock E0–E2 with no authored E3 cost.</refined_question>
</invoke>
confirmedFindings.question: Does v1's T2 reach "early E3" (needs an E3 stage + §4.7.5 cost), or does v1 cap the estate fabric at E2 with E3–E5 fully parked (then "early E3" in §3.6/§5 T2 is wrong)?

confirmedFindings.title: §3.5 nav-reveal ladder omits the R4 "Crafting tab" and R5 "Quest log" that §3.2 reveals as UI surfaces
confirmedFindings.evidence: §3.2 R4 line 1802 reveals "the **simple Crafting tab** (§2.11...) opens"; §3.2 R5 line 1803 reveals "The **Quest log** (§2.12) + the four v1 quest types". §3.5 declares it ladders "the nav chrome itself ... as its own reveal sequence (data: RevealableEntry kind:'navLink'|'screen')" (line 1955) but its table jumps Skills(R2) → Combat(R3) → **Map(R6)** → House(R7) — no entry at R4 or R5. The Skills "tab" and Combat "tab" ARE listed; the Crafting "tab" (same wording) is not.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: §3.5 is the authoritative ledger for the signature "nav reveal one-tab-at-a-time" feature (G8). If Crafting/Quests are nav entries, §3.5 is incomplete (the one-at-a-time ladder is unverifiable / a builder implementing §3.5 misses them); if they are sub-panels under an existing screen, calling them a "tab"/"log" parallel to Skills/Combat is misleading.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Either add the R4 Crafting and R5 Quest-log reveals to the §3.5 nav track (preserving one-at-a-time), or reclassify them in §3.2 as panels nested under an existing screen (not new nav entries) and adjust the wording.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Both quotes verified verbatim: §3.2 R4 (line 1802) "the simple Crafting tab (§2.11...) opens"; R5 (line 1803) "The Quest log (§2.12) + the four v1 quest types"; §3.5 (lines 1957-1967) has no R4/R5 nav entry, jumping Skills(R2)→Combat(R3)→Map(R6)→House(R7). The gap is real but partly self-reconciling: the §3.5 Combat row (line 1961) explicitly nests "drill yard, Bestiary, Equipment live under it" without separate entries, establishing that not every revealed panel is a nav-track entry, and §2.1c (line 876) lists 'tab' as a kind distinct from 'navLink'/'screen'. So §3.5 is plausibly complete-by-design (Crafting/Quests are nested panels), and §3.2's "tab"/"log" nouns are loose wording. Downgraded from medium to low: a classification/clarity nit on a signature feature, not a missing feature or hard contradiction. Needs-human because deciding whether these are top-level nav nodes (add to §3.5) or nested panels (soften §3.2 wording) is a design/classification call, not a clerical fix.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">In §3.2, the R4 "simple Crafting tab" and R5 "Quest log" are revealed using nav-like nouns ("tab"/"log"), but §3.5 — the explicit nav/screen-reveal ledger for the signature "UI as progression" feature — has no entry at R4 or R5. The §3.5 Combat row already nests Bestiary/Equipment "under" the Combat node without their own entries. Are the Crafting surface and Quest log (A) new top-level nav nodes — then §3.5 must add R4/R5 rows to keep the one-at-a-time ladder verifiable — or (B) panels nested under an existing screen (e.g. Work/Combat) — then §3.2's "tab"/"log" wording should be softened to "panel" so a §3.5 reader isn't misled?
confirmedFindings.question: Are the R4 Crafting surface and R5 Quest log new top-level nav entries (then §3.5 must list them) or panels nested under an existing screen (then §3.2's "tab"/"log" wording should be softened)?

confirmedFindings.title: R5→R6 and V2→V3: ladder table tags them RANK (Estate Service) but the earned-transition spine names an "Estate & Wealth" PILLAR threshold
confirmedFindings.evidence: §3.2 R6 trigger col (1804): "`RANK` Estate Service (drive the *shinden* + workshops to recorded yield...)" — but §3.2.1 R5→R6 "Earned by" (1840): "**Estate & Wealth** thresholds — drive the first *shinden* reclamation...". Same split at V3: §3.4 (1909) "`RANK` Estate Service (...recorded seasonal result)" vs §3.4.1 (1934) "An **Estate & Wealth** threshold...". §3.0 (1743–1744) defines RANK = the Estate Service/Combat Standing meters and PILLAR = an Influence-pillar threshold — distinct kinds. §3.9 item 1 (2206) lists only "R7, V4, V7, G2, G6, G7" as the PILLAR-mixed rungs, deliberately excluding R6 and V3.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: It is unclear whether R6/V3 gate on the Estate Service rank meter or the Estate & Wealth pillar — a difference the §4.1 thresholds and the gate predicates depend on. The two tables for the same rung disagree on trigger kind.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Pick one gate kind for these wealth-themed labour rungs and make §3.2/§3.4 (trigger col), §3.2.1/§3.4.1 (spine col), and §3.9 item 1 agree — most likely reword the spine to "an Estate-Service threshold (driven by the shinden/economy deeds)" to match the RANK tag, or promote R6/V3 to RANK+PILLAR and add them to §3.9's mixed list.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Verified verbatim. The R6 ladder row (prd.md:1804) and V3 row (1909) tag the rung gate "`RANK` Estate Service," but the matching earned-transition spine rows (1840 R5→R6, 1934 V2→V3) describe the trigger as an "**Estate & Wealth** threshold." Per §3.0 (1743-1744) RANK = the Estate Service/Combat Standing meters and PILLAR = a House Influence pillar threshold whose pillars are literally named "Arms / Estate & Wealth / Standing & Office / Name & Honour" — so "Estate & Wealth" is, by the doc's own taxonomy, a PILLAR label, making the spine wording collide with the RANK tag. §3.9 item 1 (2206) reinforces RANK as intended: it lists the PILLAR-mixed rungs as "R7, V4, V7, G2, G6, G7" and deliberately omits R6 and V3. Every other RANK-meter spine row uses meter wording ("Estate-Service threshold", "Combat-Standing threshold"); only these two wealth rungs leak the pillar name, confirming a genuine internal inconsistency rather than a stylistic choice. Downgraded to low because the intended gate kind (RANK) is recoverable from two corroborating sources and §4.1 thresholds are per-TIER (T0→T1 etc.), not per-rung, so the rung-gate ambiguity does not actually break the §4 numbers. Marked needs-human (not trivial-apply) because the fix has a real fork — reword both spines to "Estate-Service threshold" to match the RANK tag and §3.9, OR genuinely promote R6/V3 to RANK+PILLAR and add them to §3.9's mixed list — and it requires coordinated edits at both 1840 and 1934.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">R5→R6 and V2→V3 are wealth/economy rungs whose ladder-table trigger column tags them `RANK` Estate Service and whose §3.9 mixed-list excludes them, yet their earned-transition spines (lines 1840, 1934) call the trigger an "Estate & Wealth threshold" — the §3.0 name for a PILLAR. Are these two rungs gated purely on the Estate-Service RANK meter (then reword both spines to "an Estate-Service threshold, driven by the shinden/economy deeds" so the table, the spine, and §3.9 all agree), or should they actually become RANK+PILLAR gates (then also change the trigger tags at 1804/1909 and add R6/V3 to the §3.9 item-1 mixed list)?
confirmedFindings.old_string: | **R5 → R6** | **Estate & Wealth** thresholds — drive the first *shinden* reclamation + restart a workshop to a **recorded yield** |
confirmedFindings.new_string: | **R5 → R6** | An **Estate-Service** threshold — drive the first *shinden* reclamation + restart a workshop to a **recorded yield** (the economy deeds that fill the Estate-Service meter) |
confirmedFindings.question: Are R5→R6 and V2→V3 gated by the Estate-Service RANK meter (as the ladder tables and §3.9 imply) or by the Estate & Wealth PILLAR (as the §3.2.1/§3.4.1 spines say)? Both tables must then match.

confirmedFindings.title: The "tier-expansion map" reveal is placed at R6 in §3.2/§3.5 but at R7 in §1.5.1/§1.12
confirmedFindings.evidence: §3.2 R6 (1804): reveals "the **tier-expansion map** seed + the road out"; §3.5 (1962): "**"Map" screen** | `RANK` R6 ... The **first map screen** (the estate, with a road leading out)". But §1.5.1 R7 (225) lists among R7 unlocks "cash-crop levers; the **tier-expansion map**. The capstone bridge to T1." and §1.12 R7 (707): "+ the tier-expansion map (capstone bridge to T1)."
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: A reader using §1.5.1/§1.12 as the rung spec places the strategic/tier-expansion map at R7; §3.2/§3.5 place the map screen (with the road out) at R6. It is ambiguous whether these are the same object revealed at different rungs or two distinct maps (estate map at R6 vs next-tier strategic map at R7).
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Disambiguate: if the estate map (with road) is R6 and a separate next-tier strategic map is R7, say so in both places; otherwise align §1.5.1/§1.12 to the R6 placement used by §3.2/§3.5.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed verbatim. §3.2 R6 (line 1804) reveals "the tier-expansion map seed + the road out" and §3.5's nav-reveal track places the "Map" screen at RANK R6 (R7's nav reveal is instead the House/Influence screen), while §3.2's R7 row never mentions the map. By contrast §1.5.1 R7 (225) lists "cash-crop levers; the tier-expansion map. The capstone bridge to T1." and §1.12 R7 (707) lists "cash-crop levers + the tier-expansion map (capstone bridge to T1)." So the same named object is placed at R6 in the detailed/authoritative §3 ladder + nav track but at R7 in the §1 vision-level pacing summaries. The village tier itself opens at R6, so the tier-expansion map logically belongs at R6, making the R7 listings inconsistent. Not reconciled by context. Severity low (placement ambiguity in summaries vs. spine, no gameplay impact). Needs-human because resolution hinges on a design choice: one map seeded at R6 vs. a distinct next-tier strategic map at R7.
confirmedFindings.question: The detailed §3 ladder (§3.2/§3.5) reveals the tier-expansion map at R6 (the village tier opens there), but the §1.5.1/§1.12 pacing summaries list "the tier-expansion map" as an R7 reveal. Are these the same object placed inconsistently (fix: move the map mention from R7 to R6 in §1.5.1/§1.12, or note "seed at R6, full at R7"), or are there two distinct maps — an estate map with the road out at R6 and a separate next-tier strategic map at R7 — which should be named distinctly in all four places?

confirmedFindings.title: §1.7 world table reattaches the MC's origin to Kuzuhara — violates the locked 'no personal tie to Kuzuhara'
confirmedFindings.evidence: §1.7 world table line 463: "**Kuzuhara — re-foundable upstream hamlet & embankment river-works** | T2 | **Spine.** The faction-3 fusion: the drowned hamlet (the disaster that nearly killed Tahei) becomes a resettlement node ... Access-only, grind-built; the backstory and lost-child evidence resolve here." This directly contradicts the locked canon, repeated 4+ times: §5 T2.2 line 3485-3487 "*(Kuzuhara is a region node with **no personal tie to the MC's identity** — his origin/backstory and the lost-child evidence resolve through the **dream → the Sawatari-juku family** (beats 3 & 7), not here.)*"; §5 T2.5 line 3591 "per canon the MC has **no personal tie to Kuzuhara**"; §5 cross-refs line 3862-3865 "Kuzuhara has NO survivor / co-investigator character (CUT)... the MC's origin is uncovered via the **dream → the Sawatari-juku family**, never via a Kuzuhara survivor"; and prd_human_feedback.md line 129 "the **Kuzuhara-survivor NPC was CUT** (Kuzuhara = a region node only)". Where the caravan actually died is the Pass, not Kuzuhara: §5 T2.6 line 3607 / §1.7 line 468 "The High Mountains & The Pass ... the lethal terrain where his caravan died." Timeline also rules out the conflation: Kuzuhara drowned in ancestor Sadamune's generation (~50y ago; Dowager Toku, ~75, 'the only one who lived through the fall as an adult', line 499), whereas the MC's flood is recent (he is ~18-20, convalescing at the open).
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: A builder/author working the world from the §1.7 table would stage the MC's near-death disaster and resolve his backstory + the lost-child evidence AT Kuzuhara — the exact personal tie the human explicitly signed off as CUT (the Kuzuhara-survivor NPC was deleted; 'Kuzuhara = a region node only'). It is a stale leftover of the superseded spine that contradicts a hard human constraint the PRD must honour.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Rewrite the §1.7 Kuzuhara row to the locked root-sin framing: the drowned hamlet is the Kurosawa's root-sin (ancestor Sadamune's neglected flood-works); the player names the drowned and resettles it; the MC has NO personal tie — his own backstory and the lost-child evidence resolve via the Sawatari-juku family (§5 T2.5), not here.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Confirmed real. docs/prd.md line 463 (§1.7.1 world table) reads verbatim: the Kuzuhara row calls the drowned hamlet "(the disaster that nearly killed Tahei)" and says "the backstory and lost-child evidence resolve here." This directly contradicts the locked canon restated three ways: §5 T2.2 lines 3485-3487 ("Kuzuhara is a region node with no personal tie to the MC's identity — his origin/backstory and the lost-child evidence resolve through the dream → the Sawatari-juku family ... not here"); §5 T2.5 lines 3590-3593 ("per canon the MC has no personal tie to Kuzuhara"); and prd_human_feedback.md line 129 ("Kuzuhara-survivor NPC was CUT (Kuzuhara = a region node only). ✅ QA R3 #9; §2/§5 sign-off"). The conflation is also internally impossible: §1.7 line 468 places where the MC's caravan died at the Pass ("the lethal terrain where his caravan died"), and Kuzuhara drowned in ancestor Sadamune's generation (Dowager Toku, ~75, "the only one who lived through the fall as an adult," line 499) whereas the MC is ~18-20. Line 463 is a stale leftover of the superseded spine. Because the correct framing (Sadamune root-sin; name-the-drowned; resolution via the Sawatari-juku family) is already locked verbatim in §5 (lines 3481-3487, 3589-3593), aligning this summary row introduces no new story/design judgement — it propagates existing canon — so it is trivial-apply.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="verified_old">| **Kuzuhara — re-foundable upstream hamlet & embankment river-works** | T2 | **Spine.** The faction-3 fusion: the drowned hamlet (the disaster that nearly killed Tahei) becomes a resettlement node + the embankment (*seki*) river-works that secures the disaster. Access-only, grind-built; the backstory and lost-child evidence resolve here. |
confirmedFindings.new_string: | **Kuzuhara — re-foundable upstream hamlet & embankment river-works** | T2 | **Spine.** The faction-3 fusion: the drowned hamlet — the house's own **root-sin** (ancestor Sadamune's neglected flood-works) — becomes a resettlement node + the embankment (*seki*) river-works that secures the disaster. Access-only, grind-built; the player **names the drowned** and re-founds the hamlet. **No personal tie to the MC** — his origin/backstory and the lost-child evidence resolve via the **dream → the Sawatari-juku family** (§5 T2.2/T2.5), not here. |
confirmedFindings.question: Confirm the Kuzuhara framing is a region node with NO personal tie to the MC (his caravan died at the Pass; his backstory + the lost-child evidence resolve via the Sawatari-juku family). May §1.7 line 463 drop '(the disaster that nearly killed Tahei)' and 'the backstory and lost-child evidence resolve here', replacing them with the Sadamune root-sin / name-the-drowned framing used in §5?

confirmedFindings.title: §1.5.3 calls Kuzuhara the MC's 'birthplace' — stale, contradicts the no-personal-tie canon
confirmedFindings.evidence: §1.5.3 line 317: "(The re-foundable **Kuzuhara** birthplace and the wider post-town commercial region are T2+ expansion nodes — see §1.7.1.)" Calling Kuzuhara a 'birthplace' asserts a personal/origin tie to the MC, contradicting §5 T2.5 line 3591 "the MC has **no personal tie to Kuzuhara**" and prd_human_feedback.md line 129 "Kuzuhara = a region node only". 'birthplace' appears nowhere else in the PRD (grep confirms a single occurrence).
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Same stale-spine contradiction as narrative-canon-1: a reader takes 'Kuzuhara birthplace' to mean the MC was born there, reintroducing the cut personal tie.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Drop 'birthplace' — Kuzuhara is a re-foundable hamlet, not the MC's birthplace.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed at docs/prd.md:317: "(The re-foundable **Kuzuhara** birthplace and the wider post-town commercial region are T2+ expansion nodes — see §1.7.1.)". Calling Kuzuhara a "birthplace" asserts an MC origin tie that directly contradicts established canon: §5 line 3591 "the MC has **no personal tie to Kuzuhara** — it is purely a struggling, depopulated upstream hamlet", line 3485 "Kuzuhara is a region node with **no personal tie**", line 3863 "the MC has **no personal tie to Kuzuhara**", and prd_human_feedback.md:129 "Kuzuhara = a region node only". The MC's actual origin/birthplace is Sawatari-juku post-town (§1.5.3 lines 290-292), not Kuzuhara. grep confirms "birthplace" is a single occurrence in the PRD. The replacement word "hamlet" is the exact canon term used at line 3591, so the fix is purely clerical with no design judgement.
confirmedFindings.old_string: **Kuzuhara** birthplace
confirmedFindings.new_string: **Kuzuhara** hamlet

confirmedFindings.title: Two different locations are both labelled 'where he was found' — the weir vs the boundary-stone/jizō
confirmedFindings.evidence: §1.7 world table places the find-spot in two different zones, both 'where he was found': line 450 "**The Boundary-Stone / Jizō** | Village of Asagiri (T1) | Village / neutral | ... Where he was found — the **one** capped residual-ambiguity beat"; line 452 "**The River, Ford & Weir** | Satoyama & Mountains (shared, T0–T2) | Shared wilderness | ... the weir where he was found". Repeated in §5: line 3383 "**The Boundary-Stone / Jizō** — where he was found" and line 3385 "the weir where he was found". The cold open and origin canon pin it to the weir: line 3109 "pulled from the weir"; line 575 "snagged at the weir below the estate". The boundary-stone is also the anchor of the single residual-ambiguity beat (line 613 "the unidentified-hand offering at the boundary-stone where he was found").
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The lone canonical residual-ambiguity beat (the ≤1 cap, A2) depends on an offering being left where he was found. If 'where he was found' is the weir (per the cold open/origin) but the offering/ambiguity is catalogued at the village boundary-stone in a different zone, the beat's premise is geographically self-contradicting.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Pick one canonical find-spot, or explicitly co-locate them (the boundary-jizō stands at the weir/ford and marks the spot) so only one location claims 'where he was found'.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed verbatim: docs/prd.md:450 labels "The Boundary-Stone / Jizō" (Village of Asagiri, T1) as "Where he was found," while docs/prd.md:452 labels "The River, Ford & Weir" (Satoyama & Mountains, shared T0–T2) as "the weir where he was found." The same dual labelling repeats in §5 at lines 3383 and 3385. The origin canon (line ~575, "snagged at the weir below the estate") and the cold open (line ~3109, "pulled from the weir") pin the find-spot canonically to the weir. Yet the lone capped residual-ambiguity beat (the unidentified-hand offering, §1.10 ~613) is anchored to the boundary-stone "where he was found." Since the two are catalogued in different zones, the find-spot is asserted in two geographically distinct places — a genuine narrative-canon contradiction that destabilizes the premise of the single A2 ambiguity beat. Not reconciled anywhere in the text; no co-location statement exists. Fix requires a story judgement (co-locate the jizō at the weir vs. remove the label from one entry), so needs-human rather than trivial-apply.
confirmedFindings.question: Was the protagonist found at the weir or at the village boundary-stone/jizō? The origin/cold-open canon says the weir, but the lone residual-ambiguity beat (the unknown-hand offering) is anchored to the boundary-stone, and both are tagged "where he was found" in two different map zones. Choose one: (a) state that the boundary-jizō stands at the weir/ford and marks the find-spot (co-locate, keeping the offering anchored to the rescue site), or (b) remove "where he was found" from whichever location is not canonical (and re-anchor the offering accordingly).

confirmedFindings.title: Name near-collision: T0 field-lad 'Mago' vs T1 antagonist 'Magobei' — left unresolved
confirmedFindings.evidence: §1.8 line 503: "**Sota & Mago** | A grizzled groom and a cheeky teen field-labourer — the bottom-rung peers." (also line 3189 "field-lad **Mago**"). §1.8 line 527: "**Foreman Magobei** | A skimming village foreman — the 'tanuki' of the rumours board. | The **T1 antagonist** ...". 'Mago' is an exact prefix of 'Magobei'. The PRD elsewhere explicitly resolves such collisions: sweetheart Ohana→Osen and Osen/Otsuru de-rhyming (line 3859-3861), father Kuranosuke→Jinpachi (line 746-747).
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: A player who befriends bottom-rung peer 'Mago' in T0 and then meets the skimming foreman 'Magobei' (a named antagonist) in T1 can conflate them. The doc's own disambiguation standard for near-rhyming names was applied to the post-town women and the father but missed here.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Rename the minor T0 field-lad (the flavour peer) to something not sharing Magobei's stem.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified in docs/prd.md: T0 flavour peer "Mago" (lines 503, 3189) and locked T1 antagonist "Foreman Magobei" (lines 503/527/637/3321; also locked in decisions.md D-005 and brainstorms locked-decisions). "Mago" is an exact prefix of "Magobei," a genuine name near-collision. The PRD applies an explicit de-collision standard elsewhere — Kuranosuke→Jinpachi (lines 304/533/746) and sweetheart→Osen to de-rhyme from Otsuru (lines 3859-3861), both flagged as resolved — so leaving Mago/Magobei unaddressed is a real inconsistency against the doc's own standard. Downgraded medium→low: Mago is a roster flavour peer who never appears in dialogue and sits in a different tier (T0) from the antagonist (T1), who is always introduced with the full "Foreman Magobei"; confusion risk is real but minor. Needs-human because Magobei is locked canon, so the field-lad must be renamed, and choosing a period-appropriate replacement is a creative/naming decision with no clerical answer.
confirmedFindings.question: The T0 field-lad "Mago" (a flavour roster peer, never appears in dialogue — prd.md:503, 3189) shares an exact name prefix with the locked T1 antagonist "Foreman Magobei." The PRD already de-collides similar names elsewhere (Kuranosuke→Jinpachi; sweetheart→Osen vs Otsuru). Rename the field-lad to remove the prefix collision (e.g. Tasuke / Kuma / Hachi), or accept the overlap as harmless since Mago is never named in dialogue and Magobei always carries his title? Magobei is locked canon, so only the field-lad can move.

confirmedFindings.title: Origin family's grief-belief about the MC: 'dead' (§1.8) vs 'away/lost' (§3.6.2 & §5)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §1.8 cast: line 533 Jinpachi "Grieved him as dead"; line 534 Oyuki "grieved him as dead". §3.6.2 Origin ladder: line 2093 O1 "grieved-as-lost, now home"; line 2096 O4 "Father **Jinpachi** — grieved as away/lost — returns". §5 T2.5 line 3567 "The family grieved him as away/lost". So §1.8 frames the family as believing Tahei dead, while §3.6.2/§5 frame them as believing him merely away/lost.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: This is the game's 'warmest and most costly payoff' (§2.1); 'back from the dead' and 'long-absent and returned' are materially different emotional registers. Separately, the em-dash placement in line 2096 makes 'grieved as away/lost' read as Jinpachi himself (not the MC) being lost, blurring who-grieved-whom.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Pick one belief (dead vs away/lost) and apply it consistently across §1.8, §3.6.2, and §5 T2.5; clarify line 2096 so 'grieved as away/lost' clearly refers to the MC.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: duplicate-key-guard
confirmedFindings.question: Origin-family grief register: did Tahei's family at Sawatari-juku believe him DEAD (§1.8 lines 533-534, consistent with the O4 log "They said the pass took you") or merely AWAY/LOST (§3.6.2 O1/O4 and §5 T2.5)? Pick one canonical framing and apply it across all three sections. Trade-off: "presumed dead / back from the dead" is the higher-stakes, more cathartic register; "away/lost" is gentler and softer. Separately, clarify line 2096 ("Father Jinpachi — grieved as away/lost — returns") so the grief clearly attaches to the MC rather than reading as Jinpachi himself being the lost one.

confirmedFindings.title: "Character level" is load-bearing but its XP source / level curve is never defined, and the §6.4 GameState omits it
confirmedFindings.evidence: §4.6.1 line 2765 makes character level a combat input: `hpMax = 40 + 8·characterLevel + 2·STR + gearHp`. §4.4 line 2661: "**Attribute points:** **+1 point every 2 character levels**, plus milestone grants." §2.7 line 1063: "Character level + XP grants HP, satiety capacity, attribute points, and a compounding skill-XP multiplier." But §4.5 ("Per-skill XP curves") defines ONLY per-skill curves — there is no curve, XP source, or formula anywhere for *character* level or for the promised "compounding skill-XP multiplier." Worse, the canonical GameState (§6.4 lines 4048-4049) stores `character: { hp; satiety; attributePoints; attributes }` — with NO `level`/`xp` field — yet §2.7c (line 1077, "character `Level + xp`") and §2.19 (line 1604, persists "character level/xp") both say it is stored. So the variable that drives HP, the attribute-point economy, and the skill-XP multiplier is undefined as to how it rises and inconsistently treated as stored-vs-derived.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The combat HP formula, the entire attribute-point economy, and a global skill-XP multiplier all read `characterLevel`. A builder cannot implement progression, combat HP, or the mediocre-start attribute pacing without knowing how character level accrues and on what curve. This blocks M2 (combat) and the attribute system.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Decide and specify character level: either (a) DERIVE it from total skill XP (then remove it from §2.19/§6.4 'stored' and add a derivation rule + the skill-XP multiplier formula in §4.5), or (b) make it its own stored XP track (then add the `level/xp` field to §6.4's `character` object and a §4 curve). Define the 'compounding skill-XP multiplier' magnitude either way.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Verified every cited line. §4.6.1 (line 2765) reads `characterLevel` in hpMax; §4.4 (line 2661) keys attribute points off "every 2 character levels"; §2.7 (line 1063) ties HP/satiety/attribute-points plus a "compounding skill-XP multiplier" to character level. Yet §4.5 (2676-2743) defines ONLY per-skill XP curves — there is no character-level curve, XP source, or multiplier magnitude anywhere in §4. The §6.4 canonical persisted GameState (lines 4048-4049) lists `character: { hp; satiety; attributePoints; attributes }` with NO level/xp field (and the §2.8 `CombatDeedsPool.combatXp` is also absent from the stored surface), while §2.7c (line 1077) and §2.19 (line 1604) both say character level/xp IS stored — a direct stored-vs-derived contradiction. There is also unresolved terminology conflation between "character level" and the §2.8 "character Combat Level" (a CombatDeedsPool with no defined pool->level curve). The finding's claims are all confirmed; it is a real, builder-blocking gap for M2 combat HP and the attribute-point economy. Not refutable. Requires a design decision (derive vs. stored track, the curve, and the multiplier value), so needs-human, not a clerical fix.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">"Character level" drives the combat HP formula (§4.6.1 hpMax = 40 + 8·characterLevel), the attribute-point economy (§4.4 +1 per 2 levels), and a "compounding skill-XP multiplier" (§2.7) — but §4 defines only per-SKILL XP curves, never a character-level curve/source, and §6.4's canonical save omits any level/xp field while §2.7c and §2.19 both say it is stored. Decide: (a) DERIVE character level from total skill XP (then remove "level/xp" from §2.19/§6.4 'stored', add a derivation rule + the skill-XP-multiplier formula in §4.5), or (b) make it its own STORED XP track sourced from the §2.8 CombatDeedsPool/combat-XP (then add a `level`/`xp`/`combatXp` field to §6.4's `character` object and a §4 accrual curve)? Either way: (1) reconcile it with the separately-named "character Combat Level" (CombatDeedsPool), and (2) specify the magnitude of the "compounding skill-XP multiplier."
confirmedFindings.question: How does 'character level' accrue and on what curve? §4.5 only defines per-SKILL curves, yet §4.6.1 (hpMax = 40 + 8·characterLevel), §4.4 (+1 attribute point per 2 character levels) and §2.7 (HP/satiety-cap/skill-XP-multiplier per level) all depend on it. Is it (a) derived from total skill XP — so it must leave the §2.19/§6.4 save and gain a derivation rule — or (b) its own stored XP track needing a curve and a GameState field? And what is the 'compounding skill-XP multiplier' value?

confirmedFindings.title: "Combat Level" (a CombatDeedsPool) is introduced in §2.8/§1 but never defined in §4 and not stored in §6.4 — and is ambiguous against "character level"
confirmedFindings.evidence: §2.8 line 1098: "It carries a **character Combat Level** (a Combat Deeds pool: combat-XP + a deed-count of kills / clears / defends)" and line 1124: "`CombatDeedsPool { combatXp, deedCounts (kills|clears|defends), perEventCap }` → the Combat Level and a gated **Arms** contribution." §1 line 93 lists it as a carried element distinct from "weapon/martial skills." Yet the term "Combat Level" appears NOWHERE in §4 (no curve, no derivation, no mechanical effect), and §6.4 stores no `combatXp`/deeds pool — only `counts: Record<CountId, number>` (line 4060: "kills, clears, harvests"). It is also unclear whether "character Combat Level" is the same number as §2.7's "character level" (which feeds hpMax in §4.6.1).
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: A first-class combat concept is dangling: a builder cannot tell whether Combat Level is a real number to compute (and where its `combatXp` is stored), a synonym for character level, or just narrative framing for Arms accrual. Combined with finding 1, the level model has two undefined 'levels'.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Either define Combat Level as a real mechanic (give it a curve/effect in §4, add `combatXp` to §6.4), or delete the term and state plainly that combat deeds feed (i) weapon-skill levels (§2.7) and (ii) the Arms pillar (§2.16), with no separate 'Combat Level'.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Confirmed against source. docs/prd.md §2.8 line 1098 ("It carries a character Combat Level (a Combat Deeds pool: combat-XP + a deed-count of kills / clears / defends)") and line 1124 ("CombatDeedsPool { combatXp, deedCounts (kills|clears|defends), perEventCap } -> the Combat Level and a gated Arms contribution") exist verbatim, and §1 line 93 lists "character combat level" as distinct from "weapon/martial skills." A grep for "Combat Level"/"CombatDeedsPool"/"combatXp" returns hits only in §1, §2.8, and the M-milestone scaffold (line 4526) — it appears NOWHERE in §4. §4 instead defines a "Combat XP = mobLevel·COMBAT_XP_K" quantity (§4.6.5 line 2798) and a SEPARATE "Skill XP to the equipped weapon line" (line 2799), but never derives, curves, or names a "Combat Level." §6.4's GameState (lines 4041-4065) stores skills{xp}, counts (kills/clears/harvests, line 4060), and a transient combat?:CombatEncounterState (line 4062, mid-fight only), but no persistent combatXp/CombatDeedsPool. Meanwhile §2.7 line 1063 and §4.4 line 2661 reference a distinct "character level" that grants attribute points/HP. So a builder cannot tell whether "character Combat Level" is a real mechanic to compute and store, a synonym for character level, or narrative framing for the already-defined combat-XP/deed/Arms machinery. The deed-count side IS partly covered (counts -> achievements/Arms jumps, §4.6.5 line 2801), which is why severity stays medium rather than high — but the named concept and its stored field genuinely dangle. Not a clerical fix: deciding whether Combat Level is a first-class mechanic (needs curve, effect, stored combatXp) or should be deleted/re-pointed is a design call -> needs-human.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">§2.8 (lines 1098, 1124) and §1 (line 93) name a "character Combat Level" fed by a CombatDeedsPool {combatXp, deedCounts(kills|clears|defends), perEventCap}, but §4 never defines it (no curve, no effect) and §6.4 stores no combatXp/CombatDeedsPool (only skills{xp}, counts, and a transient encounter). Is "Combat Level" (a) a real, distinct mechanic — in which case define its XP->level curve and mechanical effect in §4 and add a stored combatXp/deeds pool to §6.4; (b) a synonym for §2.7/§4.4's existing "character level" — in which case say so and merge; or (c) just narrative framing for the already-defined streams (combat-XP feeding character level, weapon-skill XP, and deed-counts feeding Arms) — in which case delete the term "Combat Level" from §2.8/§1 and re-point to those? Note §4.6.5 already defines a "Combat XP" stream separate from weapon-skill XP whose storage destination is itself unspecified, so this should be resolved jointly with where that combat XP lands.</refined_question>
</invoke>
confirmedFindings.question: Is 'character Combat Level' (§2.8, from a CombatDeedsPool of combat-XP + kill/clear/defend counts) a distinct mechanic, or terminology for character level / weapon-skill level / Arms accrual? §4 never defines it and §6.4 stores no combatXp pool. If real, define its curve, effect, and stored field; if not, remove the term and re-point §2.8/§1.

confirmedFindings.title: §2.9/§2.13 mandate a SEPARATE belief-beast registry, but the §6.5 content-registry catalog has no such module
confirmedFindings.evidence: §2.9(c) line 1162-1163: "`BeliefBeast { id, rumourQuestId, resolvesToCause (human|animal|natural), oneShot: true }` — kept in a **separate registry** from grindable mobs (enforces the canon rule at the type level)." §2.13(c) line 1305 reiterates "`BeliefBeast` (shared with 2.9's separate registry)" plus the `BeliefCauseTableEntry` belief→cause table, and §2.20 line 1636 has the verifier assert the table's ≤1-ambiguity cap. But §6.5's content-registry catalog (lines 4089-4104) lists `content/enemies.ts` (grounded, "NO belief-creatures in spawn tables"), `quests.ts`, `scrolls.ts`, etc. — and NO module for belief-beasts / rumours / the belief→cause table. The canon-required separate registry has no home in the architecture catalog.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The 'no belief-creature in any spawn table' rule is a canon type-level guard (§2.22 #5) and is verifier-checked — but it can only be enforced 'at the type level' if the separate registry actually exists as a module. Its omission from §6.5 leaves the guard unrepresented in the build's data layout.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add a `content/beliefBeasts.ts` (or `rumours.ts`) registry row to §6.5 holding the belief-beast one-shots + the belief→cause table, kept apart from `enemies.ts`.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Confirmed: §2.9(c) (1162-1163), §2.13(c) (1305-1307), §2.22 #5 (1696-1697) and the §3 cross-ref (3845) all mandate a belief-beast/belief-cause registry kept SEPARATE from grindable mobs, with the Rumour / BeliefBeast / BeliefCauseTableEntry data shapes spelled out. The §6.5 catalog table (4087-4104) lists enemies.ts, scrolls.ts, quests.ts, etc. but has NO row for belief-beasts/rumours/the belief→cause table — a genuine internal inconsistency, since §6.5's own principle is 'one module per type.' Severity downgraded from medium to low because the registry is NOT actually absent from the architecture: the M2a milestone (line 4552) explicitly instructs building enemies.ts 'kept in a registry SEPARATE from an (empty/scaffold) BeliefBeast registry,' and §6.6's verifier checks already reference belief content — so the build does create it; only the enumerated §6.5 table omits it. Marked needs-human (not trivial-apply) because the fix is not purely clerical: §6.5 declares 'one module per type,' yet Rumour, BeliefBeast and BeliefCauseTableEntry are three distinct types; the auditor's proposed single bundled row (dual BeliefBeastId/RumourId key, three types in one module) and even its name ('beliefBeasts.ts (or rumours.ts)') is an undecided architecture-authoring choice that violates the stated one-type-per-module convention.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">§6.5's content-registry catalog omits the canon-mandated separate belief-beast registry (required by §2.9(c)/§2.13(c)/§2.22#5 and already scaffolded in milestone M2a). Following §6.5's 'one module per type' convention, how should the belief content be slotted into the catalog: (a) one combined module (e.g. content/lore.ts or content/rumours.ts) holding Rumour + BeliefBeast + BeliefCauseTableEntry — simplest, but bundles three types against the one-type-per-module rule; or (b) separate rows — content/beliefBeasts.ts (BeliefBeastId), content/rumours.ts (RumourId), and the belief→cause table either inline or as its own module — convention-clean but more rows? Note LoreScroll already maps to content/scrolls.ts, so it stays put. Whatever is chosen, the new module(s) should be tagged as the registry the §6.6 'no belief-creature in spawn tables' / '≤1 residual-ambiguity token' verifier checks read from.</refined_question>
</invoke>
confirmedFindings.old_string: | `content/enemies.ts` | the **grounded** bestiary (boars/wolves/monkeys/bandits/ronin/smugglers — NO belief-creatures in spawn tables, canon §E) — stats, attackSpeed, loot tables | `EnemyId` |
confirmedFindings.new_string:
| `content/enemies.ts` | the **grounded** bestiary (boars/wolves/monkeys/bandits/ronin/smugglers — NO belief-creatures in spawn tables, canon §E) — stats, attackSpeed, loot tables | `EnemyId` |
| `content/beliefBeasts.ts` | the **separate** belief-beast / rumour registry (INVESTIGATE-then-confront one-shots resolving to a human/natural cause; the belief→cause table holding the single ≤1 residual-ambiguity token) — kept apart from `enemies.ts` so no belief-creature can enter a spawn table (canon §E, §2.9/§2.13) | `BeliefBeastId` / `RumourId` |

confirmedFindings.title: §6.5's universal `Rewards` object omits `pillarDeltas`, though §2.1/§2.12 and §7's own applyRewards route pillar accrual through that bus
confirmedFindings.evidence: §2.1(c) line 879-880 defines the universal bundle WITH pillars: "`RewardBundle { items?, xp?, coin?, koku?, materials?, ... pillarDeltas? }` — the universal object every dialogue/quest/threshold/combat-deed can emit," and §2.12(d) line 1270: "The reward bus can carry `pillarDeltas` for recognized completions (achievement jumps...)." §7-M2d line 4583 likewise: "applyRewards' pillarDeltas produce capped achievement JUMPS." But §6.5 line 4107 states "Rewards are one shape everywhere" and gives `{ items?, xp?, resources?, unlocks?, areas?, recipes?, quests?, flags?, dialogues? }` — with NO pillar/influence field. So the 'one shape everywhere' object cannot carry the pillar accrual that the most load-bearing system (§2.16) depends on.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: §2.16 achievement-jump accrual is the macro spine. If the canonical reward shape in §6.5 lacks the pillar field, the architecture contradicts §2's claim that deeds funnel pillar deltas through the same bus, and a builder following §6.5 would have no path for accrual via applyRewards (which §7 explicitly requires).
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add the pillar field to the §6.5 Rewards object to match §2.1's RewardBundle.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
All quoted text verified verbatim. §6.5 line 4107's canonical "Rewards are one shape everywhere" object lists 9 fields with no pillar field, while §2.1(c) line 880 explicitly includes `pillarDeltas?` in the universal RewardBundle, §2.12(d) line 1270 says the reward bus carries `pillarDeltas`, and §7-M2 task 1 line 4583 explicitly requires "applyRewards' pillarDeltas produce capped achievement JUMPS." Pillar accrual through the reward bus is settled canon, so the §6.5 omission is a genuine internal inconsistency, not a design question. Downgraded to low severity: the mechanism is exhaustively documented in §2.1/§2.12/§4.2/§7, the §6.5 field lists are clearly illustrative (they already diverge in naming from §2.1's list), so a builder would not actually be blocked — it is a list-completeness nit in the canonical shape statement. Fix is clerical (add `pillarDeltas?` to match §2.1), no trade-off.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="verified_old">`Rewards` object (`{ items?, xp?, resources?, unlocks?, areas?, recipes?, quests?, flags?, dialogues? }`),
confirmedFindings.old_string: `{ items?, xp?, resources?, unlocks?, areas?, recipes?, quests?, flags?, dialogues? }`
confirmedFindings.new_string: `Rewards` object (`{ items?, xp?, resources?, unlocks?, areas?, recipes?, quests?, flags?, dialogues?, pillarDeltas? }`),

confirmedFindings.title: GameState has no current-location field, yet the `travel` intent writes it and §2.19 persists it
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §6.4 GameState interface (lines 4041-4065); cf. §6.3 Intent line 3990, §2.19 line 1605
confirmedFindings.evidence: The stored `GameState` (§6.4, lines 4041-4065) enumerates clock, resources, producers, skills, character, inventory, equipment, influence, tier, ranks, reputation, allegiance, flags, unlocked, quests, counts, effects, combat?, log, settings — but there is NO `currentArea`/`location` field. `unlocked: Set<SurfaceId>` is only "panels/screens/areas the player has earned" (line 4058), i.e. WHICH areas are reachable, not WHERE the player currently is. Yet §6.3 defines `| { type: 'travel'; areaId: AreaId }` (a verb whose entire job is to change current location), and §2.19's persist list (line 1605) explicitly includes "current location". Current location is non-derivable (the player chose it) and must be stored or every reload silently teleports the player to a default area.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: A save cannot resume the player where they were; the `travel` intent's effect is unpersistable. This is a concrete save/resume bug baked into the data model, and §6.4 and §2.19 directly disagree on whether location is stored.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add a `currentArea: AreaId` (set by the `travel` intent) to the stored GameState in §6.4 (and to the §6.8 persist list).
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Verified against docs/prd.md. §6.4 (lines 4041-4065) is described as "the persisted surface" and enumerates schemaVersion, rng, clock, resources, producers, skills, character, inventory, equipment, influence, tier, ranks, reputation, allegiance, flags, unlocked, quests, counts, effects, combat?, log, settings — no currentArea/location field. `unlocked: Set<SurfaceId>` (line 4058) is "panels/screens/areas the player has earned" = reachability, not current position. §6.3 line 3988 defines `| { type: 'travel'; areaId: AreaId }`, a verb that mutates current location, and §2.19's persist list (line 1605) explicitly includes "current location". Current location is player-chosen and non-derivable, so it must be stored — yet §6.4 (the canonical stored interface) omits it while §2.19 mandates it. Genuine internal contradiction and a save/resume gap.
