Downgraded high→medium: it is a real omission but §2.19 already establishes the intent to persist location and the §6.4 block is a sketch, so it would likely be caught at implementation; the fix is a no-trade-off clerical add that makes §6.4 honour the already-decided §2.19 canon, hence trivial-apply.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="verified_old">  clock: { tick: number; day: number; season: Season; year: number };  // abstract time
  resources: Record<ResourceId, number>;         // koku, coin(mon), wood, fish, materials…
confirmedFindings.old_string: clock: { tick: number; day: number; season: Season; year: number };  // abstract time
confirmedFindings.new_string:
clock: { tick: number; day: number; season: Season; year: number };  // abstract time
  currentArea: AreaId;                            // where the player IS now (set by the `travel` intent) — non-derivable, persisted (§2.19 "current location")
  resources: Record<ResourceId, number>;         // koku, coin(mon), wood, fish, materials…

confirmedFindings.title: §6.4/§6.8 stored surface omits non-derivable state that §2.19 explicitly persists (market saturation, estate stage/structures/roster, weather, equip durability)
confirmedFindings.evidence: Three sections claim to enumerate the persisted surface and disagree. §2.19 (lines 1603-1608) persists "...equip state + durability... reputation/meters... estate stage + built structures + roster, market saturation state". §2.4 defines `MarketState { perGoodPriceIndex, saturationByGood, recoveryRate }` (line 974) and §6.3 (line 4016) recovers "the market-saturation damper" per-day — path-dependent, non-derivable. §2.2 (line 911) defines `WorldClock { ... lunarPhase, weather }`. But §6.4's GameState has no market field, no estate-stage/structures/roster field, no weather field, and no per-instance durability store (equipment is just `Partial<Record<EquipSlot, ItemInstanceId>>`, line 4051; clock is `{ tick, day, season, year }`, line 4044). §6.8's persist list (lines 4169-4173) likewise stops at "...counts, active-effect remainders, settings" — omitting all of the above. Market saturation in particular is unambiguously non-derivable yet has nowhere to live.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Per §6.4's own cardinal rule ("store only what is NOT derivable"), these path-dependent systems CANNOT be recomputed on load. As written, the market damper, estate build state, weather, and item durability either reset on every reload or are unrepresentable — and the doc contradicts itself on what the save contains.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Reconcile §6.4/§6.8 with §2.19: add the genuinely non-derivable fields (at minimum `market: MarketState`; estate stage/structures/roster unless they are fully encoded by `flags`/`producers`; current weather unless it is re-derived from a day-keyed RNG sub-stream; item-instance durability). Decide explicitly which of weather/estate-stage are derived vs stored, then make all three lists agree.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Confirmed against source. §6.4's GameState (lines 4041-4065) and §6.8's persist enumeration (lines 4169-4173) both omit state that §2.19 (lines 1603-1608) explicitly persists. At least two omitted fields are unambiguously non-derivable per the PRD's own definitions: (1) market saturation — §2.4 line 974 `MarketState { perGoodPriceIndex, saturationByGood, recoveryRate }`, recovered per-day in §6.3 line 4016 (path-dependent); and (2) equipment durability — §2.10 lines 1192-1196 define `EquipState { slot → {equipDefId, durability, qualityTier} }` and `Durability { current, max, repairCost }` with durability that wears, and milestone M2b line 4553 says reduce 'applies durability wear immutably', yet §6.4 stores equipment only as `Partial<Record<EquipSlot, ItemInstanceId>>` with no instance/durability store. Both directly violate §6.4's cardinal rule ('store only what is NOT derivable') and would reset on reload as written. Weather/lunarPhase (§2.2 line 911) and estate stage/roster (§2.17) are more arguable (weather may be re-derivable from a day-keyed RNG sub-stream per §6.7; estate state may be flag-encoded), which is exactly why the reconciliation needs a design call rather than a clerical fix.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">The three 'stored surface' enumerations (§2.19, §6.4 GameState, §6.8 persist list) disagree. Market-saturation state and equipped-item durability are non-derivable per §2.4/§6.3 and §2.10/M2b respectively, but appear only in §2.19 — they have nowhere to live in §6.4's GameState. For each of {market-saturation state, equipped-item durability, current weather + lunarPhase, estate stage/built-structures/roster}: is it (a) a STORED non-derivable field that must be added to §6.4's GameState interface and §6.8's persist list, or (b) DERIVED/encoded-by-flags (weather re-derived from a day-keyed RNG sub-stream; estate stage inferred from flags/producers) and therefore should be struck from §2.19's persist list? Market saturation and durability look clearly (a); weather and estate-stage are the genuine design calls. Once decided, all three enumerations must be made to agree.</refined_question>
</invoke>
confirmedFindings.question: Which of {market-saturation state, estate stage/built-structures/roster, current weather, equipped-item durability} are truly STORED non-derivable fields (and must be added to §6.4/§6.8), versus derivable/encoded-by-flags (and should be struck from §2.19's persist list)? The three 'stored surface' enumerations currently contradict each other.

confirmedFindings.title: RNG sub-stream model in §6.7 (split from a single parent counter) contradicts the locked 'named sub-streams that never perturb each other' canon
confirmedFindings.evidence: Canon D-013 locks: "One seeded RNG = splitmix64 ... drawn through **named sub-streams** (combat / loot / seasonal / world-gen) **so systems never share or perturb each other's draws**" (decisions.md:138; echoed H4 / §6.13 #2 line 4304 "splitmix64 + named sub-streams"). But §6.7 (lines 4151-4152) says: "For independent sub-streams ... we **derive child RNGs by splitting from the parent counter**". Splitting from a single shared parent counter is order-dependent: drawing from combat advances the parent and therefore changes what weather/loot draw — the exact 'perturb each other's draws' the canon forbids. Compounding this, the persisted RNG is a single `state.rng = { seed, counter }` (§6.7 line 4146; §6.4 line 4042 "seed + counter/stream"; §6.8 line 4170 "RNG (seed + counter)"), which cannot hold the independent cursor positions of four named sub-streams. The only sub-stream with its own persisted cursor is combat (inside `CombatEncounterState`, §6.4 line 4062); loot/seasonal/world-gen have nowhere to persist an independent position.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: This breaks the locked determinism guarantee. Either sub-streams perturb each other (violating canon and making replays/saves non-reproducible across feature additions) or each needs its own persisted cursor that the `{ seed, counter }` shape doesn't provide. The whole 'byte-identical replay' / 'saved fight resumes identically' promise (lines 4025-4027, 4154-4155) rests on getting this right.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Specify named sub-streams as name-derived independent generators (e.g. `subStream(seed, name)` seeded by a hash of the stream name) and persist each long-lived sub-stream's cursor, OR explicitly document that all non-combat draws are consumed within a single tick/reduce and re-derived per (name, day/season) so only one master cursor + combat's own cursor need persisting. Then fix §6.7's 'split from the parent counter' wording and the stored RngState shape to match.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified all cited text. Canon D-013a (decisions.md:138) and the human-RESOLVED §6.13 #2 (prd.md:4302-4304) lock "named sub-streams (combat/loot/seasonal/world-gen) so systems never share or perturb each other's draws" with consequence "persist RNG seed + stream cursors" (plural). But §6.7 describes a single `state.rng = { seed, counter }` (line 4145) and "derive child RNGs by splitting from the parent counter" (lines 4151-4152), and §6.8 persists "RNG (seed + counter)" (line 4170) — one cursor, counter-keyed splitting, with the word "named sub-streams" absent from §6.7. Counter-splitting off a shared parent is order-dependent and the single-counter persisted shape cannot hold four independent named-stream cursors (only combat has its own persisted cursor, §6.4 line 4062). So the PRD's RNG model genuinely diverges from the locked canon. Real, but I downgrade severity from high to medium: a single seeded run is still fully deterministic/byte-replayable regardless of the substream mechanism; the property actually at risk is forward-stability across future content additions ("never perturb each other"), the canon's rationale but a limited practical risk for v1. Fix requires a design choice on the substream scheme, so needs-human, not a clerical edit.
confirmedFindings.question: The locked canon (D-013a) requires "named sub-streams (combat/loot/seasonal/world-gen) that never perturb each other's draws" and "persist stream cursors", but §6.7 specifies a single `{ seed, counter }` persisted state with child RNGs "split from the parent counter" (order-dependent; non-combat streams would share one cursor). Which scheme do you want pinned? (a) Each named stream carries its own persisted counter in GameState (RngState becomes a map of name->cursor; matches canon literally, larger save). (b) Streams are re-derived deterministically per (name, epoch e.g. day/season) and consumed within a single tick/reduce, so only the master cursor + combat's existing CombatEncounterState cursor need persisting (simpler save, but "non-combat draws are transient" must be guaranteed). Then §6.7's "split from the parent counter" wording and the §6.4/§6.8 `{ seed, counter }` shape need to be updated to match the chosen scheme.

confirmedFindings.title: §6.7 still offers 'mulberry32 or splitmix64' as an open choice, but splitmix64 is LOCKED
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §6.7 line 4143 vs §6.13 #2 (line 4304), H4 (prd_human_feedback.md:269), canon D-013 (decisions.md:138)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §6.7 line 4143: "A small, fast, well-distributed generator (e.g. **mulberry32** or **splitmix64**), seeded at new-game from a stored seed." But §6.13 #2 (line 4304) records "RESOLVED: RNG algorithm → splitmix64 ... locked as **splitmix64** with named sub-streams", H4 (feedback line 269) "RNG = splitmix64", and canon D-013 (decisions.md:138) "One seeded RNG = splitmix64". Offering mulberry32 as a live option contradicts the lock.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: A builder reading §6.7 first could implement mulberry32; the algorithm choice pins save reproducibility once content ships, so the stale 'e.g.' is a real foot-gun.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: State splitmix64 as fixed (the lock), drop mulberry32 as an option.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed at docs/prd.md:4142-4143. §6.7 presents the RNG as an open choice ("A small, fast, well-distributed generator (e.g. **mulberry32** or **splitmix64**)"), but the same PRD's own ADR §6.13 #2 (lines 4302-4304) records "RESOLVED: RNG algorithm → splitmix64 ... locked as **splitmix64** with named sub-streams." This is a genuine internal contradiction — the algorithm is locked elsewhere yet §6.7 still lists mulberry32 as a live alternative. Since the seed→sequence mapping pins save reproducibility once content ships, the stale "e.g." is a real foot-gun. Fix is purely clerical (align §6.7 to the already-made lock), no design judgement.
confirmedFindings.old_string:
**One seeded RNG for the entire game** (canon §H, convention *one RNG*). A small, fast, well-distributed
generator (e.g. **mulberry32** or **splitmix64**), seeded at new-game from a stored seed.
confirmedFindings.new_string:
**One seeded RNG for the entire game** (canon §H, convention *one RNG*). A small, fast, well-distributed
generator — **splitmix64** (LOCKED, §6.13 #2), seeded at new-game from a stored seed.

confirmedFindings.title: §6.4 Influence type `{ value, highWater }` omits the dent field its own comment and §6.8/§2.19 say is stored
confirmedFindings.evidence: §6.4 line 4052: `influence: Record<PillarId, { value: number; highWater: number }>;  // 4 pillars; dents recorded here` — the inline comment says "dents recorded here" but the type has no dent field. §6.8 line 4170 persists "influence pillars (value + high-water + **dents**)"; §2.19 line 1606 persists "Influence pillar values + **dents** + high-water marks"; §2.16(c) defines `Dent { pillar, amount, scriptedSourceId, recoverable }` (line 1475); and §4.2.4 (line 2602) requires "**max one dent active per pillar**" — which is unrepresentable without storing whether a dent is currently active.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The 'max one dent active per pillar' rule can't be enforced and the never-a-wipe dent state can't survive a reload if the type literally has no dent field. The type contradicts its own comment and two other persist lists.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add an active-dent slot to the stored Influence record to match §6.8/§2.19/§4.2.4.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed at docs/prd.md:4052. The §6.4 GameState `influence` type is `Record<PillarId, { value: number; highWater: number }>` with the inline comment "dents recorded here" — but there is no dent field. This contradicts (a) its own comment, (b) §6.8 line ~4170 which persists "influence pillars (value + high-water + dents)", (c) §2.19 line ~1606 which persists "Influence pillar values + dents + high-water marks", and (d) §4.2.4 which mandates "max one dent active per pillar" (unrepresentable from value/highWater alone). The Dent type is already defined in §2.16(c) as `{ pillar, amount, scriptedSourceId, recoverable }`, and the ≤1-active rule makes `Dent | null` per pillar the correct shape. The fix honors clearly stated, multiply-corroborated intent and introduces no new design/balance judgement — clerical.
confirmedFindings.old_string: influence: Record<PillarId, { value: number; highWater: number }>;  // 4 pillars; dents recorded here
confirmedFindings.new_string: influence: Record<PillarId, { value: number; highWater: number; dent: Dent | null }>;  // 4 pillars; value + high-water + the (≤1) active recoverable dent (§4.2.4) — matches §6.8 / §2.19

confirmedFindings.title: How `tier` advances is under-specified: §6.13 says 'set by the tier-up intent', but the Intent union has no such verb and §6.3/§2.16 describe it as automatic
confirmedFindings.evidence: §6.13 #4 (line 4310-4311): "Store `tier` as the committed value (**set by the tier-up intent**) and treat threshold-progress as derived". But the enumerated Intent union (§6.3 lines 3985-3998) lists no `tier_up`/`advance_tier` variant (it has rake_rice, do_activity, travel, buy_producer, equip, set_stance, use_item, combat_action, advance_dialogue, accept_quest, read_scroll, set_allegiance + "...one variant per verb"). Meanwhile §6.3 reduce (lines 4010-4011) says it "re-checks unlock and **tier-threshold predicates** so newly-earned surfaces flip to unlocked", and §2.16(b) (lines 1467-1468) describes advancement as automatic: "cross a tier's required-pillar thresholds **and** trigger its story gate → the next tier's canvas opens". So it is unclear whether tier-up is an explicit player intent or an automatic reduce/tick consequence — and the verb that §6.13 says writes the stored `tier` is missing from the contract.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: `tier` is the only stored, story-bearing macro field (it fires rewards/log). Whether it is written by an explicit confirmable intent or auto-flipped on threshold-crossing changes the reducer contract, the DEV-API surface, and how the tier-up story beat is gated — and the §6.3 contract doesn't name the mechanism.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Either add an explicit `advance_tier` variant to the Intent union and state that reduce only computes tier-threshold PROGRESS (player confirms the tier-up), or state that tick/reduce auto-advances tier when thresholds+story-gate are met and remove the 'tier-up intent' wording from §6.13. Make §6.3, §6.13, and §2.16(b) agree.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed verbatim. §6.13 #4 (prd.md:4310-4311) says `tier` is "set by the tier-up intent", but the Intent discriminated union (3985-3998) defines no tier_up/advance_tier variant — only "...one variant per verb" as a non-exhaustive caveat. The §6.3 reduce text (4009-4011) describes reduce re-checking "tier-threshold predicates so newly-earned surfaces flip to unlocked" (automatic unlock surfacing) but never describes writing state.tier, and §2.16(b) (1466-1468) frames advancement as automatic-on-conditions ("cross thresholds AND trigger its story gate → next tier opens"). So the write-site of the only stored, story-bearing macro field is genuinely unspecified: it is unclear whether tier-up is an explicit player-dispatched intent (with confirmation) or an auto-commit consequence inside reduce when the story gate fires and thresholds are met. The phrase "tier-up intent" occurs exactly once in the entire repo and names a verb the contract never defines. Real ambiguity. Downgraded to low (not medium): §2.16(b) does materially constrain the mechanism — both the threshold-crossing AND a player-triggered story gate are required, so a workable reading is that "tier-up intent" is loose shorthand for the story-gate-completing intent (a quest/dialogue verb) whose processing in reduce commits the tier. Impact is on spec clarity / reducer-contract design at M0, not on shipped behaviour. Resolving it requires a design choice, so needs-human.
confirmedFindings.question: How is state.tier committed, and which contract element writes it? Option A: add an explicit `advance_tier`/`confirm_promotion` variant to the §6.3 Intent union — reduce only computes tier-threshold PROGRESS, and the player explicitly confirms the promotion (gives a deliberate story beat + DEV-API hook). Option B: no dedicated intent — reduce auto-commits the next tier the moment required-pillar thresholds are met AND the tier's story-gate flag is set (set by completing the gate quest via the existing advance_dialogue/accept_quest verbs); then remove the "tier-up intent" wording from §6.13 and have §6.3 reduce explicitly state it commits state.tier. Whichever is chosen, §6.3 (Intent union + reduce text), §6.13 #4, and §2.16(b) must be made to name the same mechanism and write-site.

confirmedFindings.title: §7 milestones + risk register entirely omit the FUN dimension and its fun-proxy instrumentation, despite fun being a first-class v1 quality bar
confirmedFindings.evidence: A full-text scan of §7 (lines 4316–4833) finds ZERO occurrences of 'fun', 'dead-time', 'engagement', 'slog', 'playtest', 'fun-factor', 'qa-playtesting', 'reward cadence', or 'always-a-visible-next-goal'. §7 instruments PACING exhaustively (M1 'per-rung ≥30-min-floor pacing instrumentation', M6 'headless pacing-instrumentation harness') but never the FUN-PROXIES. The risk register §7.4.1 lists exactly five rows — R1 scope-creep, R2 balance-tuning-time, R3 save-migration, R4 art/feel, R5 combat-density — none covering 'the 28.5 h of grind ships balanced but reads as a slog / has dead-time / no visible next goal'. This contradicts CLAUDE.md ('QA fun & visuals, not just function… A compiling build isn't the bar — the game must be paced and fun') and prd_human_feedback K2 (line 344: 'the PRD has pacing targets but no FUN proxies — these are the two real gaps') and K3 (line 350: the fun-proxy loop runs 'M0–M3… instrument headless fun-proxies… via the DEV play-API'). The fun-factor.md and qa-playtesting.md living docs exist, but §7 never wires their fun-proxy measurement into any milestone task and never names fun as a risk.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Fun is the project's single most-emphasized quality bar and the explicitly-named #1 gap in the human feedback; a 24-rung, ~28.5 h grind with a ≥30-min-per-rung floor makes 'engaging vs. tedious' the top product risk. A milestone roadmap that claims to deliver 'everything v1 requires' but instruments only time-budgets and visuals will hit verify-green without ever measuring or de-risking whether the grind is fun.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add fun to §7 in two places: (a) a §7.4.1 risk row ('R6 — the locked grind reads as a slog / dead-time' → mitigation: the M0–M3 vertical-slice + headless fun-proxy instrumentation per fun-factor.md & qa-playtesting.md, agent-driven playtest loop, human final call); and (b) explicit fun-proxy instrumentation tasks (dead-time, reward/unlock cadence, always-a-visible-next-goal, first-5-min hook) in M1/M3/M6 alongside the pacing harness, cross-referencing docs/fun-factor.md and docs/plans/qa-playtesting.md.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified against source. §7 (4316–4833) contains zero fun-proxy instrumentation: M6 (4662–4696), the dedicated QA/polish milestone, instruments pacing exhaustively (headless per-rung tick→wall-clock harness, ≥30-min floor, locked tier hour budgets), accessibility, art, and a capture-game-states VISUAL sweep — but never the fun-proxies (dead-time / reward-cadence / always-a-visible-next-goal / first-5-min hook) and never cross-refs docs/fun-factor.md or docs/plans/qa-playtesting.md. The risk register §7.4.1 has exactly five rows (R1 scope-creep, R2 balance-time, R3 save-migration, R4 art/feel, R5 combat-density); none covers 'the locked ~28.5 h grind ships balanced but reads as a slog / has dead-time / no visible next goal.' The intent is explicit and load-bearing: prd_human_feedback.md K2 (line 344-345) calls FUN proxies one of 'the two real gaps,' and K3 (346–351) specifies the exact headless fun-proxy loop driven via the DEV play-API; CLAUDE.md makes fun a first-class v1 bar ('a compiling build isn't the bar — the game must be paced and fun'). The DEV play-API (§6.10) and the two living docs provide the capability, but §7's milestone roadmap and risk register never wire fun-proxy measurement into any task nor name fun as a risk — a genuine gap, not reconciled by context. Fix requires design/scope judgement (which proxies, which milestones, a new risk row), so needs-human.
confirmedFindings.question: §7's milestone roadmap (esp. M6) and the §7.4.1 risk register instrument PACING (time budgets) and VISUALS (capture-game-states sweep) but never the FUN-proxies (dead-time, reward/unlock cadence, always-a-visible-next-goal, first-5-min hook) that prd_human_feedback K2/K3 and CLAUDE.md make a first-class v1 bar — and fun is never named as a risk. Should §7 be amended to (a) add a risk row 'R6 — the locked ~28.5 h grind ships balanced but reads as a slog / has dead-time / no visible next goal' (mitigation: M0–M3 vertical-slice + headless fun-proxy instrumentation per fun-factor.md & qa-playtesting.md, agent-driven playtest loop, human final call), and (b) add explicit fun-proxy instrumentation tasks to M1/M3/M6 alongside the pacing harness, cross-referencing docs/fun-factor.md and docs/plans/qa-playtesting.md? Trade-off: more roadmap surface and a build-plan commitment to measure fun vs. leaving the project's top quality bar owned only by living docs and never gated by the milestone plan.

confirmedFindings.title: §7.1.2 pacing acceptance criterion omits the human-blessed R0 cold-open exemption (would falsely FAIL the pacing test) and labels the local gate 'CI' despite the locked 'no hosted CI' constraint
confirmedFindings.evidence: §7.1.2 states the floor as '≥ ~30-min-per-rank floor (no single rung advances faster)' and the test as 'a headless playthrough that clears any rung in < ~28 min … fails the pacing test'. But §4.8.1 (lines 2972–2977) explicitly carves out R0: 'The ≥30-min per-rank floor… applies to the 7 grind rungs R1–R7; R0 is the exempt ~5-min cold-open story rung', and §4.8.1's R0 row is '~5 min'. Taken literally, the §7.1.2 test ('any rung in < ~28 min … fails') would FAIL on R0 (~5 min). Separately, 'CI-enforced acceptance criteria' contradicts H7 / §7.3 (line 4763: 'no hosted CI, no deploy automation') and §7.2 M6 phase 3 (line 4691: 'the harness assertions run inside npm run verify') — there is no CI; the gate is the local verify run. M6 phase 2 (line 4690) itself correctly scopes the floor to 'every grind rung ≥~30 min'.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: This is the acceptance-criterion text a builder turns into the M6 pacing-regression assertion; as written it would either reject the (correct, ~5 min) R0 cold-open or force the author to silently diverge from §7.1.2. The 'CI' label compounds the confusion against the explicit no-hosted-CI lock.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Scope the pacing test to grind rungs (R1–R7 / V0–V7 / G0–G7), exempt R0 per §4.8.1, and relabel 'CI-enforced' as 'verify-gate-enforced' to match §7.3.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Both sub-claims verified verbatim. §7.1.2 (line 4391) phrases the acceptance criterion as "clears any rung in < ~28 min ... fails the pacing test"; read literally this catches R0 (~5 min, §4.8.1 line 2954), contradicting the explicit human-blessed carve-out in §4.8.1 lines 2972-2977 ("applies to the 7 grind rungs R1–R7; R0 is the exempt ~5-min cold-open story rung"). Separately, "CI-enforced acceptance criteria" (line 4390) conflicts with the hard lock "no hosted CI, no deploy automation — confirmed by the human 2026-06-25" (line 4763) and the resolved "local verify gate, no hosted CI" (line 4827); M6 phase 3 (line 4691) and the §7.3 gate correctly use the local-verify framing. Downgraded to low: the R0 carve-out and no-CI lock are clearly documented elsewhere and §4.8.4 already scopes the budget table to grind rungs, so a careful builder would not actually ship a false-failing test — but the canonical §7.1.2 text is genuinely loose and self-inconsistent. Fix is clerical alignment to already-locked decisions (R0 exempt, no hosted CI), no design/balance/scope judgement, so trivial-apply.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="verified_old">per-tier hour budgets and the ≥30-min floor as **CI-enforced acceptance criteria** (a headless
playthrough that clears any rung in < ~28 min, or misses a tier budget, **fails the pacing test** —
§4.8.4, §6.10). Tuning retunes *yields*, never these targets.
confirmedFindings.old_string:
per-tier hour budgets and the ≥30-min floor as **CI-enforced acceptance criteria** (a headless
playthrough that clears any rung in < ~28 min, or misses a tier budget, **fails the pacing test** —
§4.8.4, §6.10). Tuning retunes *yields*, never these targets.
confirmedFindings.new_string:
per-tier hour budgets and the ≥30-min floor as **verify-gate-enforced acceptance criteria** (a headless
playthrough that clears any **grind** rung — R1–R7 and the V0–V7 / G0–G7 equivalents; the **R0 cold-open
story rung is exempt** per §4.8.1 — in < ~28 min, or misses a tier budget, **fails the pacing test** —
§4.8.4, §6.10). Tuning retunes *yields*, never these targets.

confirmedFindings.title: The fourth Influence pillar (Name & Honour) has no milestone that owns its 'bar first lights/scores' reveal, though the four-bar panel is defined to reveal bar-by-bar
confirmedFindings.evidence: §7.1.1 (line 4380) ships 'the four pillars… the four-bar panel'. M3 phase 5 (line 4587) reveals the panel 'revealing ONE bar at a time as each pillar first scores (Estate from the shinden, Arms from R6's recorded standing, **Office/Name shown faint/not-yet-scored**)'. Office's first-light is then explicitly owned by M4 ('the Office bar first lights at V4', line 4609; M4 phase 6 'the Standing & Office bar lights', line 4619). But NO milestone assigns Name & Honour's first-light moment. Per §4.2.2 (line 2510) Name's seasonal budget is JUDGE_K = 4,500 at T1 (0 at T0), and Name = sqrt of (armsHW+estateHW+officeHW)/TIER_REF_NAME, so Name first scores at the first T1 season — early in M4 — yet M4 is silent on it; M5 (T2) never mentions it either.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Three of the four bars (Estate, Arms in M3; Office in M4) have a defined, tested 'first scores' beat; the fourth does not, so the 'reveal bar-by-bar as each pillar first scores' contract has a hole and no milestone test asserts when/where the Name bar transitions from faint to lit.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Assign Name & Honour's first-light to M4 (it scores at the first T1 seasonal high-water mark per §4.2.2) and add it to M4's phases/DoD the way Office's V4 first-light is called out.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Confirmed against source. §4.2.2 (prd.md:2505-2510) gives Name & Honour a seasonal JUDGE_K of '—' at T0 but 4,500 at T1, so Name first scores in the first T1 season (inside M4); its basis armsHW+estateHW+officeHW is already nonzero entering T1 (Arms/Estate carried from T0), so Name would in fact light before Office's V4 first-light. M3 phase 5 (4587) and R7 (1805) mark Office AND Name 'faint/not-yet-scored' at end of T0. M4 DoD (4609) and M4 phase 6 (4619) explicitly own and unit-test Office's V4 first-light ('Office first lights exactly at V4') but assign Name's first-light to no milestone, and M5 never mentions it. So the 'reveal bar-by-bar as each pillar first scores' contract leaves Name's faint->lit transition unowned and untested, with an unaddressed ordering wrinkle (Name lighting before Office). Real gap. Downgraded medium->low: the four bars are all revealed at R7 (two lit, two faint) so the structural panel reveal is covered; only the Name faint->lit beat and its ordering are missing. Needs-human because deciding when Name lights (and whether before/after Office) is a design/ordering choice, not a clerical fix.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">The four-bar panel reveals each bar 'as the pillar first scores,' and Estate/Arms (M3) and Office (M4-V4) each have an owned, unit-tested first-light beat. Per §4.2.2, Name & Honour first scores in the first T1 season (JUDGE_K becomes 4,500 at T1; basis armsHW+estateHW+officeHW is already >0 from T0) — which would light Name's bar early in M4, before Office's V4 light. M4 currently never owns or tests this. Should we (a) assign Name's first-light to M4 with a test asserting it lights at the first T1 seasonal high-water mark (accepting it lights before Office at V4), or (b) intentionally hold Name faint through v1 / defer its first-light to a later beat — and if held faint, reconcile that with §4.2.2 which already has Name scoring at T1?
confirmedFindings.question: The four-bar panel reveals bar-by-bar 'as each pillar first scores', but only Estate/Arms (M3) and Office (M4-V4) have a defined first-light beat. §4.2.2 has Name & Honour first scoring at the first T1 season — should M4 explicitly own the Name bar's first-light (and assert it), or is Name intended to stay 'faint' through v1?

confirmedFindings.title: Fishing — a LOCKED v1 skill — has no milestone that builds it, and its introduction timing is contradictory (§2.6 says T0 R5–R6; the ford only opens at T1-V2)
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §7.1.1 (4378), §7.2 M1 phase 1 (4494) & M4 phase 2 (4615); cf. §2.6 (1051), §3.2 R2 ladder (no ford in T0)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §7.1.1 (line 4378) lists 'farming · foraging · woodcutting · **fishing** · smithing · cooking' as the LOCKED v1 skill set. But no milestone phase introduces fishing: M1's registries author 'the ~4 T0 labour skills' and its labour loop is explicitly 'farming → foraging → woodcutting → hauling' (lines 4478, 4494) with areas 'Kura, Gate & Forecourt, Home Paddies, Stables & Woodlot Edge, Near Satoyama' — no ford. M2a/M2b are combat; M3 (R4–R7) covers cooking and smithing but never fishing. The fishing node first appears in the roadmap at M4 phase 2: 'V2 … opens the Ford/Weir' (line 4615). Yet §2.6 (line 1051) states 'fishing fold in across **R5–R6** and the wilderness rings' (i.e. T0), and the §3.2 T0 ladder (R0–R7) contains no ford/weir at all (only Near Satoyama / Stables & Woodlot Edge).
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: A locked core skill is unassigned to any build task, and the design and the roadmap disagree on whether it comes online in T0 (R5–R6, per §2.6) or T1 (V2, where the ford actually opens) — a builder cannot tell which milestone owns it or when the player first fishes.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Decide fishing's home tier (most evidence points to T1-V2 where the Ford/Weir opens) and either (a) correct §2.6's 'R5–R6' to the V-rung, or (b) add a T0 ford/fishing reveal to M3; then name fishing explicitly in that milestone's phases.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Verified against docs/prd.md. Fishing is a LOCKED v1 skill (§7.1.1, line 4378) but no milestone phase authors a fishing activity/skill: M1's labour skills are farming/foraging/woodcutting/hauling (lines 4478, 4494), M2a/M2b are combat, and M3 (R4-R7) covers cooking/smithing/quests but never fishing. The §3.2 T0 ladder reveals no ford/weir and never names fishing. The introduction timing is genuinely contradictory three ways: §2.6(e) line 1051 says fishing folds in at R5-R6 (T0); §1.7 line 452 calls the Ford/Weir 'T0-T1, gated by conditioning. Fishing...'; but §3.4 V2 (line 1908) and the roadmap M4 phase 2 (line 4615) actually OPEN the Ford/Weir at T1-V2 (and even there only as a HUNT/CLEAR combat ring, with no fishing activity authored). A builder cannot tell which milestone owns fishing or when the player first fishes. Severity medium: a locked core skill is unassigned and the design self-contradicts on its tier home, but fishing is a minor labour node and the ford does open concretely at V2. Needs-human because resolving it requires a design/scope decision (pick the tier and reconcile §1.7/§2.6 with §3.4/M4), not a clerical edit.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">Fishing is a LOCKED v1 skill but no milestone phase builds it, and the PRD gives three conflicting introduction times: §2.6(e) says fishing folds in at T0 R5-R6 with the wilderness rings; §1.7 tags the River/Ford/Weir as 'T0-T1'; but §3.4 V2 and the roadmap (M4 phase 2) only open the Ford/Weir at T1-V2 (and there only as a HUNT/CLEAR combat ring, with no fishing activity authored). Decide fishing's home tier and assign it to a build phase. Option A: it's T1 — correct §2.6's 'R5-R6' to V2, tighten §1.7 to 'T1', and add 'author the ford fishing node/skill' explicitly to an M4 phase. Option B: it's T0 — add a T0 ford/fishing reveal to the §3.2 ladder (e.g. R5-R6) and name fishing in an M1 or M3 phase, and reconcile §3.4/M4 so the V2 'opens the Ford/Weir' reads as combat-ring expansion of an already-fishable spot. Which tier owns fishing, and which milestone phase authors the fishing activity/skill?
confirmedFindings.question: Fishing is a locked v1 skill but no milestone builds it, and §2.6 (T0 R5–R6) conflicts with the roadmap (Ford/Weir opens at T1-V2). Where does fishing come online — T0 (add the ford to M3) or T1 (fix §2.6 and keep it in M4-V2) — and which milestone phase owns it?

confirmedFindings.title: The rich attribute system (STR/AGI/INT/SPD/LUCK + character level + attribute-point allocation) is consumed by combat in M2a but never explicitly built by any milestone phase
confirmedFindings.evidence: The attribute system is load-bearing (D13; §2.7 'the rich attribute system… STR/AGI/INT/SPD/a luck stat'; §2.7(e) line 1086 'T0 — attributes exist from the open'; §4.4 line 2661 '+1 point every 2 character levels'). M1 'Lands' nominally cites §2.7, but its phases build only SKILLS: phase 2 'grants per-skill XP… derives **levels** from the §4.5.1 curve' (line 4495) — skill levels, not character level/attributes. M2a then CONSUMES attributes as a combat input without building them: phase 1 'baseSpeed + SPD coefficient' / phase 3 'a post-drills MC (~weaponSkill 5 / **attrs 10**)' (lines 4526, 4528). No milestone phase builds the character-XP/level loop that grants HP, satiety capacity, and attribute points (§2.7c), nor the attribute-point allocation surface, nor the milestone-perk web.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: A major, human-locked system (and the §4.4 'no dump stat / no respec' contract) is silently assumed to exist by M2a's combat math but is never given a build task — a builder following the phase lists would reach M2a with no attributes implemented.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add explicit phase(s) for the attribute system + character level/XP + attribute-point allocation, owned by M1 (or the front of M2a, since combat depends on it), mirroring how skills are detailed in M1 phase 2.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Verified verbatim: §2.7(a/c/e) (l.1059-1086) defines the rich attribute system + character-level/XP loop (grants HP, satiety capacity, attribute points) + milestone-perk web, with "T0 — attributes exist from the open"; §4.4 (l.2661) sets "+1 point every 2 character levels" under a human-LOCKED no-respec/no-dump-stat contract; §4.6.1 (l.2758-2765) makes the combatant model intrinsically depend on STR/AGI/SPD/LUCK and characterLevel. M2a phase 1 (l.4526) consumes "baseSpeed + SPD coefficient" and phase 3 (l.4528) asserts "attrs 10". Yet a full grep of the milestone block (M0-M7, l.4417-4720) shows no phase that builds a GameState attribute store, the character-level/XP loop (distinct from M1 phase-2 SKILL XP, l.4495), the +1pt/2-levels allocation surface, or the §4.5.3 milestone perks. M0's GameState surface (l.4465: vitals/resources/flags/unlocked/log) and M1's registry list (l.4494) omit attributes entirely; M2a phase 1 only builds the Combatant type + "minimal Combatant fixtures." A load-bearing, human-locked v1 system is consumed by M2a combat math but given no build task. Not reconciled elsewhere. Needs-human because owning-milestone choice (M1 vs head of M2a) and whether the allocation/perk surface ships then are real scope/sequencing trade-offs, not clerical.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">The §2.7/§4.4 attribute system — 5 attributes (STR/AGI/INT/SPD/LUCK), a character-level/XP loop that grants HP + satiety capacity + attribute points (+1pt/2 levels), the point-allocation UI, and the §4.5.3 milestone-perk web — is consumed by M2a's combat math (SPD coefficient, hpMax's characterLevel term, the post-drills "attrs 10" win-rate assertion) but no milestone phase builds it. Canon says "attributes exist from T0" and the no-respec rule is human-LOCKED, so it is in v1 scope. Which milestone should own the build, and how much ships when? Options: (a) M1 owns the full system (attributes visible/allocatable from T0, mirroring how M1 phase 2 details skills) — but allocation has little to bite on before combat; (b) M1 stands up the GameState attribute store + character-level/XP loop (so HP/satiety are real), and M2a's head adds the combat-feeding read + allocation UI at the first consumer; (c) defer the allocation surface and milestone perks to a later milestone, with M2a hardcoding MC attrs in fixtures only. Whichever is chosen, add explicit phase(s) so a builder following the phase lists doesn't reach M2a with no attribute system implemented.
confirmedFindings.question: §2.7/§4.4's attribute system (5 attributes, character level, +1pt/2 levels, allocation UI, milestone perks) is assumed by M2a's combat numbers but never built in any milestone's phases. Which milestone should own it — M1 (attributes exist from T0) or the head of M2a (combat's first consumer) — and should the allocation/perk surface ship then too?

confirmedFindings.title: The emotional climax (Tama/Otsuru + Tahei's true name) is documented as BOTH a guaranteed spine beat AND a payoff that only lands on the fully-optional, skippable Origin track
confirmedFindings.evidence: §3.6.2 line 2088: "The **Tama-payoff** (Otsuru) and **Tahei claiming his true name** land on this track's capstone." and the O5 row (line 2097) gates it on "`RANK` Origin Ties (the track completes; coincides with the G6 spine beat)" — i.e. the OPTIONAL Origin meter being maxed. The milestone (line 4658) demands the verifier assert the Origin track "NEVER appears as a spine trigger". BUT §5 T2.7 line 3628 calls it "**[THREAD: Tama]** — the spine's personal payoff (also a main-quest beat...)" and §5 T2.2 line 3491 lists "**The lost-child truth resolves (alliance-broker). [THREAD: Tama] — PAYOFF.**" as a main-quest beat — and the §3.6 G6 SPINE log line (line 2027) already narrates it verbatim: "she is real, and grown, and not you. 'Tama ran. Tama lived.' You have a name again: Tahei."
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: This is the game's warmest, most-anticipated payoff and the stated emotional peak of the 16h tier (fun-factor §7 T2). The PRD cannot decide whether every player gets it at G6 on the spine, or only players who fully complete the optional, zero-reward Origin side-track. A speed-optimizing player who ignores Origin would, per §3.6.2/M6, never reach O5 and so never see the climax — yet the spine G6 reveal text fires it for everyone. A builder cannot implement both, and the difference is the difference between a guaranteed and a missable ending.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Decide and state once: is the Tama/Otsuru truth and the Tahei-name beat (a) spine-guaranteed at G6 regardless of Origin Ties, with the optional Origin track only adding depth/warmth (reunions, Osen, Jinpachi); or (b) genuinely gated behind completing O5 and therefore officially missable. Then make §3.6 G6, §3.6.2 O5, §5 T2.2/T2.7, and the M6 verifier rule agree, and ensure the payoff fires exactly once.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed against source. The PRD places the Tama/Otsuru reveal + Tahei's true name in two mutually exclusive locations with different gates. §1.5.3 (line 303), §3.6 overview (line 2007), and §3.6.2 (lines 2088, 2097) explicitly state the Tama-payoff lands on the OPTIONAL Origin track's capstone O5, whose trigger is 'RANK Origin Ties (the track completes)' — i.e. it requires maxing the fully-optional, zero-reward Origin meter and 'NEVER gates the spine.' Yet §5 T2.2 beat 7 (line 3491) lists it as a MAIN-QUEST beat ('PAYOFF'), §5 T2.7 (line 3628) calls it 'the spine's personal payoff (also a main-quest beat),' and the §3.6 G6 spine-ladder row (line 2027) narrates it verbatim in its guaranteed diegetic log line. Mechanically the two gates differ: G6 fires on an Office/Story threshold (spine), O5 on an Origin-Ties threshold (optional). A spine-only/min-maxing player reaches G6 without O5, so the climax cannot be both spine-guaranteed and Origin-gated. The 'coincides with the G6 spine beat' wording acknowledges but does not resolve which gate actually fires the payoff. This determines whether the game's stated emotional peak is guaranteed for all players or officially missable — a real design decision a builder cannot make alone, so needs-human rather than a clerical reword.
confirmedFindings.question: The Tama/Otsuru truth reveal and Tahei claiming his true name is the stated emotional climax of T2, but the PRD gates it two incompatible ways. Decide ONE and make §1.5.3, §3.6 (overview + G6 row), §3.6.2 (line 2088 + O5 row), §5 T2.2 beat 7, §5 T2.7, and the M5/M6 verifier rule all agree: (A) SPINE-GUARANTEED — the Otsuru reveal + name-claim fire at G6 on the Office/Story gate for every player regardless of Origin Ties, and the optional Origin track (O0-O4 reunions: Oyuki, Okimi, Denbei, Kanta, Osen, Jinpachi) only adds depth/warmth; or (B) ORIGIN-GATED — the climax genuinely requires completing the optional O5 (Origin Ties maxed) and is therefore officially missable by a spine-only player. Trade-off: (A) protects the game's warmest payoff for all players but weakens the 'doubly-earned, never free' Origin framing and makes O5's 'RANK Origin Ties' trigger partly cosmetic; (B) preserves the earned-ness but lets a min-maxer skip the game's emotional heart. If (A), the O5 row should re-cast its capstone as the Origin REUNION completing (warmth/buff) while the Otsuru/name beat is owned by the spine at G6 and merely echoed when O5 also completes.

confirmedFindings.title: T2's promised 'new verbs' (brokering / diplomacy / arbitration) are specified only as narrative reskins of the deed→ip loop — risking the 'same loop, bigger numbers' fun-killer the doc names as the slump
confirmedFindings.evidence: fun-factor §5b line 221–222: "**Layers are new VERBS, not bigger numbers.** Each tier must hand the player a new *thing to do*, not a ×10 version of the old thing... If a tier is 'same loop, bigger numbers,' that *is* the slump." T2 names its new verbs as "brokering/diplomacy verbs + the rival contest" (fun §7 T2, line 291), but §3.6's G3–G6 (arbitrate valleys, river-works, break roost, broker alliances) are described narratively, and §4.8.3 reduces them to ip-bearing deeds (e.g. line 3017 G6: "alliance Office/Name deeds"). No §3/§4 text establishes that brokering/arbitration are a mechanically distinct verb from a T0 labour deed or a T1 trade deed — they may all resolve as 'do deed → pillar jump.'
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Active-only fun requires a real decision, not a reskinned click (fun-factor §4 lever 4, §6 'choice-deserts'). If T2's headline verbs are narratively distinct but mechanically the same deed→ip jump as T0/T1, the longest tier becomes 'same loop, bigger numbers' — precisely the rot the fun doc warns kills retention. The PRD asserts the cure (new verbs) without specifying the mechanic that delivers it.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Specify what makes T2's brokering/diplomacy/arbitration mechanically distinct from earlier deeds (e.g. a contested-meter mini-game, a play-rivals-against-each-other choice with real trade-offs, a multi-step supply puzzle) — or explicitly accept they are narrative reskins and compensate with other novelty. Don't leave 'new verbs' as an unbacked claim for the slump tier.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Partially holds but materially overstated. TRUE kernel: in the pacing tables the social verbs resolve as deeds (§4.8.3 line 3017 "alliance Office/Name deeds"; line 3014 "out-supply/arbitrate; Estate Service"), and "contested meters" is named (§3.6 G3 line 2024) but never given a systems-level interaction spec in §2 — so brokering/diplomacy/arbitration are deed-framed. REFUTED parts of the claim: (a) it is NOT "only narrative reskins" — the PRD repeatedly specifies a real trade-off decision, rivals "played against each other (money vs precedence)" with Tomita-on-money vs Akagi-on-honour, at §1.5.2 (547), §1.11 (638), §3.6 G3 (2024), §5 T2.3 (3523, 3535) — exactly the play-rivals choice the finding's own resolution requests; (b) T2 carries genuinely distinct NEW mechanics beyond the social verbs (sekisho/pass travel G1, region-scale human combat + Hanzaki duel + CLEAR/CAPTURE-with-mercy branch G5, the river-works mega-lever G4, the Origin O0–O5 side-track); (c) the anti-slump guarantee does not depend on these verbs being mechanically novel — fun-factor §5d/§6 spreads it across four independent levers (front-loaded trade-economy verb at G0, seasonal twists rotating optimal strategy, re-running early loops at new scale, cross-pillar combos). No docs contradiction; the residual is a minor design-completeness gap requiring a design call, so needs-human at low severity.
confirmedFindings.question: For T2's social headline verbs (brokering / diplomacy / arbitration), do we want a distinct systems-level interaction — e.g. a defined "contested meter" resolution where supplying a valley vs restoring Akagi's precedence are competing spends with real trade-offs — or do we accept they resolve as Office/Estate deeds (§4.8.3) and rely on the already-specified play-rivals-against-each-other choice (money vs precedence) plus the §5d anti-slump levers (front-loaded trade verb, seasonal strategy rotation, cross-pillar combos) to carry T2's freshness? If the latter, no change; if the former, §2.15 needs a short "contested meter" mechanic spec that §3.6 G3/G6 can reference.

confirmedFindings.title: "Conditioning" is called labour-built / ZERO-combat-bonus in §1, but §4.5.3 lets its "combat facet" grant STR/AGI — the killed cross-feed reappears
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §1.5.1 line 202, §2.8 line 1104, §1.13 line 1364 vs §4.5.3 line 2730
confirmedFindings.evidence: Pillars/contract repeatedly fix conditioning as labour-built and combat-stat-free: §1.5.1 L202 "**Labour conditioning is a one-way enablement gate** on combat rungs (it unlocks them; it grants ZERO combat stat bonus)"; §2.8 L1104 "gated behind labour-built **conditioning** (a one-way enablement gate that grants **ZERO combat stat or training-rate bonus**)"; §1.2 pillar 4 L96 "capacity is earned through labour-built conditioning, with **no labour→combat training-rate cross-feed**." But §4.5.3 L2730 classifies conditioning as a COMBAT skill that may grant combat attributes: "Only **combat-skill** milestones (weapon lines, conditioning's combat facet) may grant STR/AGI." If conditioning is labour-built AND its combat facet grants STR/AGI, that IS the labour→combat cross-feed the doc says is "killed outright" (§1.13 L743). If instead conditioning's combat facet is combat-built, then calling it "labour-built conditioning" everywhere in §1 is wrong. Either reading is a contradiction in a binding constraint.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: This is the load-bearing mediocre-start / no-hidden-edge invariant (A3, D-011, D-013). A builder encoding §4.5.3's type-level wall would let conditioning grant STR/AGI, silently re-opening the exact soft hidden edge §1.13 claims to have killed.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Decide and state unambiguously what "conditioning" is: (a) a single labour-built enablement gate that grants ZERO combat stats (then remove "conditioning's combat facet" from the STR/AGI grant list in §4.5.3 L2730), or (b) split into two clearly-named things — a labour-built ZERO-bonus gate vs. a combat-category drill skill that may grant STR/AGI — and stop calling the latter "labour-built."
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified all quotes verbatim in docs/prd.md. §1.2 L96, §1.5.1 L202, §2.8 L1104, and §1.13 L742 repeatedly and explicitly fix conditioning as "labour-built," a "one-way enablement gate" granting "ZERO combat stat or training-rate bonus." But §4.5.3 L2730 — inside the type-level no-cross-feed wall — lists "conditioning's combat facet" as a combat-skill milestone that "may grant STR/AGI." These cannot both hold if conditioning is one object: if conditioning is labour-built and its facet grants STR/AGI, that is exactly the labour→combat cross-feed §1.13 claims to kill; if its combat facet is combat-built, then calling it "labour-built conditioning" throughout §1/§2.8 is wrong. The locked canon (brainstorms L169-170 groups conditioning with weapon lines) and decisions.md D-009 ("combat conditioning is earned independently of labour") add to the ambiguity rather than resolving it. This is a real internal inconsistency in a load-bearing, type-level-enforced invariant (D-011/D-013/A3): a builder encoding L2730 literally could type conditioning as "combat" and silently re-open the cross-feed. Not trivial-apply because the fix requires a design decision about what conditioning structurally is (single labour gate vs. split labour-gate + combat drill skill), with type-system consequences — not a clerical edit.
confirmedFindings.question: What is "conditioning," exactly? Option (a): a SINGLE labour-built enablement gate that grants ZERO combat stats — in which case §4.5.3 L2730 must drop "conditioning's combat facet" from the STR/AGI grant list, leaving only "weapon lines," and conditioning must be typed non-combat in the no-cross-feed wall. Option (b): a combat-category drill skill that legitimately grants STR/AGI — in which case §1.2/§1.5.1/§2.8/§1.13 must stop calling it "labour-built" and stop asserting it grants ZERO combat stat. Option (c): genuinely two things — a labour-built ZERO-bonus capacity gate AND a separately-named combat drill skill — which must be given distinct names so the type-level wall is unambiguous. They cannot all be "labour-built conditioning grants ZERO combat stat" AND "conditioning's combat facet may grant STR/AGI" simultaneously without re-opening the exact cross-feed the PRD says it killed.

confirmedFindings.title: Undefined "character level" XP source + freely-allocatable attribute points = an unclosed labour→combat back door the §4.5.3 wall doesn't cover
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §2.7 line 1063, §4.4 line 2661 vs §2.8 line 1098; §4.5 (no character-XP curve)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §2.7(a) L1063: "**Character level + XP grants HP, satiety capacity, attribute points**, and a compounding skill-XP multiplier." §4.4 L2661: "**Attribute points:** **+1 point every 2 character levels**, plus milestone grants" — with no constraint on allocating them to combat-feed STR/AGI. The no-cross-feed wall (§4.5.3 L2723-2731) only constrains MILESTONE grants and the labour-skill→attribute field; it never touches these free per-level points. Crucially, the PRD never defines how this "character level" gains XP: §4.5 defines only per-SKILL curves, and §2.8 L1098 separately defines a combat-sourced "**character Combat Level** (a Combat Deeds pool: combat-xp + a deed-count)". So it is undefined whether the attribute-point-granting "character level" is the combat-sourced Combat Level or a labour-inclusive aggregate.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: If the character level aggregates labour-skill XP (the dominant, labour-plurality activity), then grinding peaceful labour funds +1/2-level points that the player can pour into STR/AGI — exactly the labour→combat soft edge §1.13 claims to kill, bypassing the carefully type-enforced milestone wall. The terminology also drifts ('character level' vs 'character Combat Level').
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Define the character-level XP source explicitly, and state whether the per-level attribute points may fund combat-feed attributes. If labour XP feeds the character level, either constrain those points (cannot raise STR/AGI from labour-sourced levels) or explicitly accept it and update the no-cross-feed wording so it isn't contradicted.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed in source. §2.7(a) L1063 and §4.4 L2661 grant freely-allocatable attribute points ("+1 point every 2 character levels") from a "character level" whose XP source the PRD never defines. §4.5 defines only per-skill curves and §4.6.5 defines combat XP feeding a structurally separate "character Combat Level" / CombatDeedsPool (§2.8 L1098, §2.8c L1124) — so "character level" and "character Combat Level" are two distinct, unreconciled terms. The no-cross-feed wall (§4.5.3 L2723-2733) is scoped ONLY to the L10 milestone +1-attribute perk; it never constrains the per-2-level free points, which have no stated allocation limit and can fund STR/AGI. If the character level aggregates labour-skill XP, this is an indirect labour→character-XP→attribute-point→STR/AGI path that bypasses the type-level wall and sits in tension with the LOCKED canon "no labour→combat cross-feed" (D-009, §1.13, and §4.4's own assertion). The "it's just the combat Level under a loose name" refutation fails because §2.7a also has this level grant a general all-skill XP multiplier + HP/satiety, which reads as general progression and would be an odd combat→labour feed under that reading. Genuine underspecification, resolvable either way by an implementer — a real gap. Downgraded to medium (modest magnitude; intent partly recoverable since §4.4 already asserts no cross-feed) and kept needs-human since defining the XP source and the points' allocation rule is a design/balance decision touching a locked pillar.
confirmedFindings.question: The "character level" that grants +1 attribute point every 2 levels (§2.7a/§4.4 L2661) has no defined XP source, and is named differently from the combat-sourced "character Combat Level" (§2.8 L1098). Decide: (1) Is there ONE character level or TWO (a general one + the Combat Deeds pool)? (2) What feeds the attribute-point-granting character level — combat XP only, or an aggregate that includes labour-skill XP? (3) If labour XP feeds it, may its free per-2-level points raise combat-feed STR/AGI? Options: (a) make the character level combat-sourced only (no labour back-door; tidy the §2.7/§4.4 wording to "Combat Level" or define a separate non-labour source); or (b) make it a labour-inclusive aggregate but extend the §4.5.3 no-cross-feed wall so labour-funded levels cannot raise STR/AGI; or (c) explicitly accept labour→STR/AGI via character levels and revise the canon "no labour→combat cross-feed" wording (D-009/§1.13/§4.4) so it isn't contradicted. The current text leaves it undefined, and the verifier that is supposed to enforce the no-cross-feed invariant has nothing to check against.

confirmedFindings.title: §5 first-fight beat is survived "by Jūbei's arrival" — contradicts the canon "never rescued" mediocre-start beat
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §5 T0.2 line 3141 vs §2.8 line 1102 and §3.2 line 1801
confirmedFindings.evidence: §5 T0.2 beat 4 L3141: "is thrashed — disarmed, ribs cracked, left in the dirt — surviving **only** by luck / by drillmaster **Jūbei's** arrival, **never by skill.**" — making Jūbei's arrival a survival/rescue cause. But §2.8 L1102 fixes the opposite as canon: "never *rescued*: you survive it, THEN beg Jūbei for drills"; and §3.2 R3 L1801: "a wolf at the grain store — survived by luck, **never rescued, never skill**". The §5 line also self-contradicts its own next sentence ("The shame of limping home to confess drives him to beg Jūbei" — implying he was alone, not rescued).
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The 'survive on your own, then choose to train' framing is the perseverance beat of the mediocre-start contract; a rescue undercuts it and is the lone outlier against two canon statements.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Bring §5 in line with the 'never rescued' canon — drop Jūbei's arrival as a survival cause.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Confirmed verbatim: docs/prd.md §5 T0.2 beat 4 (L3141) lists "by drillmaster Jūbei's arrival" as a survival cause, which is a rescue framing. This contradicts two canon statements that established the opposite — §2.8 L1102 ("never rescued: you survive it, THEN beg Jūbei for drills") and §3.2 R3 L1801 ("survived by luck, never rescued, never skill"). The §5 line also self-contradicts its own following sentence ("The shame of limping home to confess drives him to beg Jūbei"), which presumes he was alone, not rescued. The mediocre-start/perseverance pillar is stated canon twice over, so aligning §5 to it requires no design judgement — purely clerical.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="verified_old">surviving **only** by luck / by drillmaster **Jūbei's** arrival, **never by skill.**
confirmedFindings.old_string: surviving **only** by luck / by drillmaster **Jūbei's** arrival, **never by skill.**
confirmedFindings.new_string: surviving **only** by luck and sheer stubbornness, **never by skill** — and **never rescued**: he lives through it on his own.

confirmedFindings.title: Origin side-track is called "an accelerant ... with ZERO mechanical gift" in the same binding clause — a literal contradiction
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §3.6.2 line 2082 (also §3.6 line 2006, §3.6.1 line 299); cf. lines 2085, 2102
confirmedFindings.evidence: §3.6.2 "Hard rules (binding)" L2081-2082: "The Origin track is **OPTIONAL, fully completable, an accelerant, narrative-only with ZERO mechanical gift**, and **NEVER gates the spine**". Yet the same track "shaves a felt-but-small slice off the climb (folded into the ~10–15% side-faction speedup)" (L2102) and yields a "pride/morale buff" (L2085). A ~10–15% time-to-tier accelerant plus a morale buff IS a mechanical effect, so "an accelerant ... with ZERO mechanical gift" is self-contradictory. The intended scope is ZERO gift FROM THE BACKSTORY/identity reveal (L2086: "never a retroactive gift from remembering"). The imprecise phrasing recurs verbatim at L299 and L2006.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: §6.6 turns canon into machine-checked invariants; an engineer encoding 'Origin track grants ZERO mechanical gift' as an assertion would conflict with the side-faction speedup and morale buff. It also muddies the pillar-6 ZERO-bonus rule by over-scoping it.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Scope the phrase to the identity reveal, e.g. across all three occurrences: "an accelerant (the small side-faction speedup + a present-day morale buff), narrative-only, with ZERO mechanical gift from the backstory/identity reveal (no stat/recipe/tool/combat bonus from remembering)."
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed verbatim at all three cited spots (docs/prd.md L299 §1.5.3, L2006 §3.6, L2081-2082 §3.6.2 "Hard rules (binding)"): "an accelerant, narrative-only with ZERO mechanical gift". The finding is correct that this over-scopes: the same track gives the ~10-15% time-to-next-tier speedup (§1.5.4 L319) and §1.5.3 L298 literally calls the pride/morale payoff "a modest global skill-XP buff" — an unambiguous mechanical bonus. So "ZERO mechanical gift" read literally is contradicted by the doc's own text. The PRD's actual intent is narrower and is stated repeatedly with the correct scope: "never a retroactive gift from remembering" and "returning memory grants access only ... and ZERO mechanical bonus" (Hard guardrail, L313). Because that qualifier sits adjacent at every occurrence, an attentive reader reconciles it; this is terminological imprecision rather than a true design conflict, hence severity downgraded medium->low. Still real and worth fixing because §6.6 turns canon into machine-checked invariants and the unqualified phrase could be encoded wrong, and because "modest global skill-XP buff" vs "ZERO mechanical gift" is an in-section tension. Not trivial-apply: the fix requires design-aware rewording across three occurrences and a decision on how to phrase the scope (and whether to keep the skill-XP-buff framing), so it needs human sign-off.
confirmedFindings.question: The Origin track is repeatedly called "an accelerant ... with ZERO mechanical gift," yet it grants the ~10-15% side-faction speedup (§1.5.4) and "a modest global skill-XP buff" (§1.5.3) — both mechanical. Confirm the intended scope: the track DOES grant the earned present-day speedup + skill-XP/morale buff, and "ZERO mechanical gift" means only "no retroactive stat/recipe/tool/combat bonus from the backstory/identity reveal (from remembering)." If so, should all three occurrences (L299, L2006, L2082) be reworded to qualify the phrase, e.g. "an accelerant (the small earned side-faction speedup + present-day morale buff), narrative-only, with ZERO mechanical gift from the backstory/identity reveal"? Separately: is the "modest global skill-XP buff" wording intentional (it is itself a mechanical bonus and should perhaps be reconciled with the ZERO-gift language)?

confirmedFindings.title: Real Edo district "Nihonbashi" used in T4 prose — a real place name not on the §6.6 fictional-setting allow-list
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §1.7.1 line 470 and §5 T4.2 line 3772 vs A6/§1.3 line 133 and §6.6 allow-list line 4127-4128
confirmedFindings.evidence: A6/§1.3 L133-134: setting "kept **fictional** — no real place or daimyō names." §6.6 L4127-4128 enumerates the exact naturalized exonyms exempted: "*shogun, ronin, yokai, samurai, Osaka, daimyō*" — Edo (the era/ceiling anchor) and Osaka are deliberately allowed, but "Nihonbashi" is NOT listed. Yet T4 prose uses it twice: §1.7.1 L470 "the **Nihonbashi/*banzuke*** payoff"; §5 T4.2 L3772 "a Nihonbashi *tonya* sends a single trial order." Nihonbashi is a specific real Edo district, a finer-grained real place name than the macro anchors the allow-list sanctions.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The fictional-setting rule is a hard human constraint (A6) and §6.6 makes the exonym set a machine-checked allow-list; leaving Nihonbashi in shipped text either trips that lint or quietly widens A6 by precedent.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Either add Nihonbashi to the §6.6 allow-list (treating it like Edo/Osaka) or genericize it (e.g. "a capital *tonya*" / "the capital's *banzuke*").
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed: "Nihonbashi" (a real Edo district) appears twice in T4 prose (docs/prd.md L470 "the Nihonbashi/banzuke payoff"; L3772 "a Nihonbashi tonya sends a single trial order"), while A6/§1.3 L133 says the setting is "kept fictional — no real place or daimyō names" and the §6.6 allow-list (L4127-4128) enumerates only shogun, ronin, yokai, samurai, Osaka, daimyō. So Nihonbashi sits outside the explicit allow-list — the core observation holds. One auditor claim is inaccurate, though: the §6.6 allow-list is the macron/plain-ASCII-romaji lint allow-list, and Nihonbashi has no macron, so it would NOT actually trip that verifier — there is no machine-checked "real place name" lint. The real issue is the soft A6 intent constraint. It is a genuine but borderline canon loose end (the PRD already uses Edo and Osaka, both real, as sanctioned macro anchors, and Nihonbashi is the commercial heart of the already-allowed Edo, so it may be covered in spirit). Resolving it requires a story/canon judgment, so needs-human, low severity.
confirmedFindings.question: "Nihonbashi" (a real Edo district) appears in two T4 prose beats but is not on the §6.6 exonym allow-list, which currently sanctions Edo and Osaka as real macro anchors. Since Nihonbashi is a district of the already-allowed Edo, do you want to (a) add "Nihonbashi" to the §6.6 allow-list and treat it as a macro anchor like Edo/Osaka, or (b) genericize it ("a capital tonya" / "the capital's banzuke") to keep A6's "no real place names" tight? Note: it would not trip the existing macron lint either way; this is a canon-consistency call, not a build-failing one.

confirmedFindings.title: Three canon-doc links over-climb the path (../../brainstorms) and 404
confirmedFindings.evidence: prd.md sits at docs/prd.md; the canon doc sits at brainstorms/2026-06-25-locked-decisions.md (repo root), so the correct relative path is ../brainstorms/... . Line 34 proves the author knows this: it correctly links `[`../brainstorms/2026-06-25-locked-decisions.md`](../brainstorms/2026-06-25-locked-decisions.md)`. But lines 1715, 2225, 4319 each write `[`../../brainstorms/2026-06-25-locked-decisions.md`](../../brainstorms/2026-06-25-locked-decisions.md)` — one ../ too many. Verified: `ls docs/../../brainstorms/2026-06-25-locked-decisions.md` → No such file or directory.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: These are the section preambles that tell a reader 'the binding canon lives here'; the link is dead, so a reader (or a tool resolving links) cannot reach the source of truth from §3, §4, or §7.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Replace every `../../brainstorms/2026-06-25-locked-decisions.md` (both the backtick display text and the href, 6 substrings across the 3 lines) with `../brainstorms/2026-06-25-locked-decisions.md`. The already-correct line 34 (`../brainstorms/...`) is untouched because it does not contain the `../../` form.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Verified against the filesystem. prd.md is at docs/prd.md, so a relative path from it climbs one level to repo root: ../brainstorms/2026-06-25-locked-decisions.md resolves (file exists, 28083 bytes). The ../../ form over-climbs above the repo root and 404s (ls docs/../../brainstorms/... -> No such file or directory). Grep confirms line 34 correctly uses ../brainstorms/ while lines 1715, 2225, 4319 each use the broken ../../brainstorms/ form twice (display text + href). Pure clerical dead-link fix, no design judgement. A replace_all of the 6 ../../ substrings (line 34's ../ form lacks the ../../ prefix so is untouched) is safe and apply-ready.</parameter>

confirmedFindings.title: §4.7.5 parks estate stages E3–E5 with a pointer to §1.5.2 (the Village web), not the estate section
confirmedFindings.evidence: Line 2911: "...keep building gated on standing, never the capstone (§1.5.1). E3–E5 parked (§1.5.2). **Levers:** ...". §1.5.2 is "VILLAGE of Asagiri (SIDE — a static reputation web)" (line 265) — it has nothing to do with estate physical stages. The E3–E5 parking and the estate-growth rule live in §1.5.1 (line 261: "Stages E3–E5 ... are parked (§1.7.1)") — which line 2911 even cites two words earlier — and §7.1.3 line 4401 parks them via §1.5.1.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: A reader chasing 'where are E3–E5 parked / explained' is sent to the unrelated Village reputation section.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Point the parking note at §1.5.1 (the estate section that defines and parks the stages), matching the adjacent citation on the same line.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified: docs/prd.md line 2911 reads "E3–E5 parked (§1.5.2)." but §1.5.2 (line 265) is "VILLAGE of Asagiri (SIDE — a static reputation web)", unrelated to estate physical stages. The estate stages E0–E2 and the parking of E3–E5 live in §1.5.1 (line 261: "Stages E3–E5 (fortified seat → restored-and-surpassed) are parked (§1.7.1)"). The same sentence even cites §1.5.1 two words earlier for the capstone gating, confirming §1.5.2 is a typo for §1.5.1. Clerical cross-ref fix, no design judgement. Downgraded to low (single mis-pointed reference, easily self-correctable by a reader).

confirmedFindings.title: Estate-stage E3 named both "Fortified Seat" (§1.5.1/§7.1.3) and "Prosperous" (§5)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §1.5.1 line 261–262: "Stages E3–E5 (fortified seat → restored-and-surpassed) are parked"; §7.1.3 line 4401 repeats "Estate stages E3–E5 (fortified seat → restored-and-surpassed)". But §5 names the stages individually and consistently otherwise: line 3668 "Estate stage span: E3 Prosperous → E4 Fortified Seat", line 3704 header "E3 → E4", line 3800 "E5 Restored-and-Surpassed". So per §5 the full set is E0 Foreclosure's Edge / E1 Stabilising / E2 Recovering / E3 Prosperous / E4 Fortified Seat / E5 Restored-and-Surpassed — meaning the E3–E5 span starts at "Prosperous", not "Fortified Seat". The range descriptors in §1.5.1/§7.1.3 mislabel the E3 endpoint (and silently drop the name "Prosperous"). The names appear nowhere in the canon/feedback/ADR docs, so this is a self-contained PRD inconsistency.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: A reader cross-checking the parked-stages list against the §5 roadmap sees E3 called two different things; whichever is wrong, the stage table is internally contradictory (this is parked T3+ flavour, so contained).
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Pick the canonical E3 name and make the §1.5.1/§7.1.3 range descriptor agree with §5's per-stage names. If §5's "E3 Prosperous → E4 Fortified Seat" is canonical, change the two descriptors to "(prosperous → restored-and-surpassed)".
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Confirmed internal inconsistency. §5 explicitly names the estate stages per-stage: line 3668 "Estate stage span: E3 Prosperous → E4 Fortified Seat" and line 3800 "E5 Restored-and-Surpassed". But the E3–E5 span descriptor "(fortified seat → restored-and-surpassed)" uses "fortified seat" (E4's name per §5) as the E3 endpoint, dropping "Prosperous". This descriptor recurs in THREE places, not two as cited: lines 261–262 (§1.5.1), line 3561 (§5 T2.4 estate-growth note — missed by the finding), and line 4401 (§7.1.3 parked table). The names appear nowhere in canon/feedback/ADR docs, confirming a self-contained PRD inconsistency. Severity downgraded medium→low: this is parked, post-v1 (T3+) flavour content with no gameplay impact. Fix class is needs-human, not trivial-apply: resolving it requires choosing which naming is canonical (E3 "Prosperous" per §5 vs "fortified seat" per the three descriptors) — a small story/flavour judgement — and touches three edit sites, so it is not a single byte-exact clerical swap.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">Estate stage E3 is named inconsistently. §5 (T3 roadmap, line 3668) explicitly names E3 "Prosperous" and E4 "Fortified Seat" (E5 "Restored-and-Surpassed"), but the parked-span summaries in §1.5.1 (line 261), §5 T2.4 (line 3561), and §7.1.3 (line 4401) all describe the E3–E5 span as "(fortified seat → restored-and-surpassed)" — starting at "fortified seat", which is E4's name. Which is canonical? Option A (likely): keep §5's per-stage names (E3 Prosperous, E4 Fortified Seat, E5 Restored-and-Surpassed) and change all three range descriptors to "(prosperous → restored-and-surpassed)". Option B: make E3 "Fortified Seat" canonical and fix §5 instead. This is parked T3+ flavour, so either way the impact is contained.</refined_question>
</invoke>
confirmedFindings.question: What are the canonical names of estate stages E3–E5? §5 explicitly names E3 "Prosperous" and E4 "Fortified Seat", but the §1.5.1 / §7.1.3 range summaries call the start of E3–E5 "fortified seat". Confirm the per-stage names so the descriptors can be aligned (likely: E3 Prosperous, E4 Fortified Seat, E5 Restored-and-Surpassed).

confirmedFindings.title: ADR D-008 still records the rejected kanji 政威 for the Standing & Office pillar (PRD uses 官威)
confirmedFindings.location: docs/history/decisions.md line 81 (D-008 Decision) vs docs/prd.md §1.6.1 line 378/386 and §5/§3 (官威 everywhere)
confirmedFindings.evidence: The PRD is internally consistent that the Standing & Office kanji is 官威 (kan'i) and repeatedly notes "the earlier coined 政威 was rejected" (prd.md lines 378, 386–387, 752, 801, 1702, 2191, 3839, 3850). The PRD's §1 decisions explicitly map to D-008. But the cited ADR, decisions.md D-008 line 81, still reads "**Standing & Office (政威)**" with no amendment note — unlike D-009, which got an explicit amendment line (decisions.md line 91) for its own renames. So the cited ADR's literal text contradicts the PRD claim it underpins.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: decisions.md is the canonical ADR log; leaving the rejected coinage in D-008 with no note means a future reader treating the ADR as source-of-truth would resurrect 政威, which the §5 authenticity pass killed.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add an amendment note to D-008 mirroring D-009's pattern, recording 政威 → 官威 and pointing to PRD §1.6.1 for current kanji.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed: docs/history/decisions.md line 81 (D-008 Decision) still lists the Standing & Office pillar as 政威, while docs/prd.md has resolved it to 官威 throughout (lines 378, 386-387, 752-753, 801-802, 1436, 1702-1703, 2191, 3838-3839, 3850) and repeatedly records that the coined 政威 was rejected at the §5 authenticity pass (2026-06-25). D-009 (line 91) already carries an explicit amendment note for its own renames, and D-013's consequence line (161) references the resolved 官威 glyph subset, so D-008 is the lone stale spot. Since decisions.md is the canonical ADR log and the PRD's §1 decisions map onto these ADRs, a reader treating D-008 as source-of-truth could resurrect the rejected coinage. Fix is a clerical amendment note mirroring D-009; the kanji decision is already locked, so no design judgement is required. Note: the actual fix target is docs/history/decisions.md (the D-008 Consequences line at 83), not docs/prd.md, which is itself fully consistent.

confirmedFindings.title: "goshi" (郷士) ships with no macron — violates the LOCKED proper-Hepburn convention and the §6.6 macron-lint
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md lines 46, 185, 755, 804, 1787, 1821, 3108, 3511 (10 occurrences; 0 with macron); also ADR D-007 (decisions.md:74) and brainstorms/2026-06-25-locked-decisions.md:40,204,292
confirmedFindings.evidence: §1.5.1 header (185): "### 1.5.1 ESTATE — the Kurosawa *goshi* household"; §1.4 (46): "lower-samurai (*goshi*) house two generations past its grandeur". The macron lock (805): "Romanization convention = macrons (proper Hepburn) project-wide (LOCKED — Tōkichi, Yagōemon, Jūbei…)" and the §6.6 lint (4125-4128): "the verifier flags plain-ASCII romaji that should carry a macron… Allow-list: …shogun, ronin, yokai, samurai, Osaka, daimyō". 郷士 is *gōshi* (long ō); "goshi" is a plain-ASCII slip and is NOT on the allow-list — exactly what the lint is meant to catch. The term appears 10× in prd.md, always unmacroned (gōshi: 0×).
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: This is the single most-repeated faction/world term (the Kurosawa house is "a *goshi* house" throughout). Authored content/display strings copied from the spec will either ship inconsistently or be flagged by the very macron-lint the PRD mandates. Fixing the spec keeps the canonical spelling correct at the source.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: replace_all "*goshi*" → "*gōshi*" across docs/prd.md (10 occurrences), and sweep the same fix into ADR D-007 (decisions.md) and brainstorms/2026-06-25-locked-decisions.md so canon matches. (Alternative, if the team prefers the un-macroned form: add "goshi" to the §6.6 allow-list — but that needs a human to bless an explicit Hepburn exception.)
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Verified against docs/prd.md: "goshi" appears 10x (lines 46, 125, 185, 546, 755, 804, 1787, 1821, 3108, 3511), all un-macroned; "gōshi" appears 0x. 郷士 reads ごうし (gōshi, long ō), so the LOCKED proper-Hepburn/macron convention (prd.md:805) calls for "gōshi". The §6.6 allow-list (prd.md:4127) covers only naturalized English exonyms (shogun, ronin, yokai, samurai, Osaka, daimyō) — gōshi does not qualify, so the locked rule does point to the macroned form. The claim is correct. Downgraded to low (not medium) because the §6.6 lint targets shipped display/name strings in code, not PRD prose, so nothing is currently flagged or broken; this is source-canon hygiene. Marked needs-human rather than trivial-apply because the term is spelled identically un-macroned across frozen canon too (ADR D-007 in decisions.md and brainstorms/2026-06-25-locked-decisions.md), so a correct fix is a coordinated multi-file sweep touching frozen ADR canon, and there is a genuine policy choice (macronize project-wide vs. bless "goshi"/"gōshi" as an explicit Hepburn exception) that the human should ratify so prd.md, the ADR log, and the brainstorm canon stay in agreement.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">The world term 郷士 (correct Hepburn: *gōshi*, long ō) is written un-macroned as "goshi" in 10 places in docs/prd.md AND in frozen canon (ADR D-007 in docs/history/decisions.md, brainstorms/2026-06-25-locked-decisions.md), contradicting the LOCKED "macrons project-wide" convention. Pick one and apply consistently across all three files: (A) replace every "goshi" → "gōshi" (honor the locked macron convention — the default, since gōshi isn't a naturalized English exonym); or (B) keep "goshi" and add it as an explicit §6.6 allow-list exception (treat it as house style). Option A is the convention-consistent choice; Option B requires blessing a Hepburn exception.</refined_question>
</invoke>
